What boy has a turtle in his bed? Title: How to Download Windows Live Messenger Windows Live Messenger is an instant messaging client developed by Microsoft as part of its Windows Live suite. This tutorial will guide you through the process of downloading this software onto your computer. By following these steps, you'll be able to chat with friends, share files, and even make voice or video calls. **Step 1: Check System Requirements** Before you start downloading, ensure that your system meets the minimum requirements for installing Windows Live Messenger. Here are the basic needs: - Operating System: Windows XP SP2 or later versions (32-bit or 64-bit) - Processor: 500 MHz processor or higher - Memory: 256 MB RAM or more - Hard Disk Space: At least 1 GB free disk space **Step 2: Go to the Official Microsoft Website** Open your web browser and navigate to the official Microsoft website at www.microsoft.com. It's important to download from here because third-party sites may contain harmful viruses or malware. **Step 3: Search for Windows Essentials** Hover over the 'Products' tab located near the top right corner of the page. A dropdown menu will appear; select 'Software & Apps'. From there, search for 'Windows Essentials'. Click on it when it appears in the search results. **Step 4: Initiate Download** Once you reach the Windows Essentials download page, click on the 'Download now' button under the appropriate language option. The file size is approximately 150MB so depending on your internet speed, it might take some time to complete. **Step 5: Run Downloaded File** After the download finishes, locate the executable (.exe) file usually found in the 'Downloads' folder. Double-click on this file to run the setup wizard. If prompted by User Account Control, choose 'Yes' to proceed. **Step 6: Agree to Terms and Conditions** Read through the License Agreement carefully before selecting 'I accept' to continue. Remember, understanding what you agree to protects your rights as a user. **Step 7: Customize Installation Options** Choose 'Customize' during installation to select only the components you want. For just Windows Live Messenger, uncheck all boxes except 'Messenger'. However, consider keeping others like Mail, Writer, Photo Gallery, Movie Maker, etc., they could also prove useful. **Step 8: Choose Install Location** Unless you have specific reasons, leave the default install location unchanged. Changing the path can lead to issues if other programs reference these files. **Step 9: Begin Installation** Click 'Install' after customizing settings according to your preference. Wait while the installer copies files and sets up shortcuts. **Step 10: Sign In or Create Account** Launch the application once installed. If you already have a Microsoft account (Hotmail, Outlook, Xbox Live), enter those credentials. Otherwise, create a new one which is quick and easy. **Key Tips:** - Always keep your antivirus software updated to prevent any potential threats during download or installation. - Regularly update your Windows Live Messenger to enjoy new features and improved security. - Be cautious about sharing personal information online. By following these simple yet detailed steps, you should now successfully have downloaded and installed Windows Live Messenger on your PC! Dairy cattle are kept and raised specially for milking. Herds of cows are kept and are regularly mated with a bull, so that they produce calves. This keeps the milk supply going. However, most commercial dairy farms do not keep bulls because of the concern that such bulls are very dangerous when being handled. Instead, cows are artificially inseminated with bull semen that is stored kept frozen in liquid nitrogen, and is "bred" by a person who artificially inseminates cows for a living. Title: How to Draw a Smurf **Introduction:** Drawing can be a fun and relaxing activity that allows you to express your creativity. This tutorial will guide you through drawing a classic character from the beloved comic series - The Smurfs! By following these steps, you'll learn how to draw Papa Smurf, one of the most iconic characters in the franchise. **Step 1: Sketching the Basic Shape** Start by sketching an oval shape for the head and a small circle underneath it for the body. These shapes don't need to be perfect; they serve as guides for positioning features later on. Remember, this is just a rough draft, so keep your lines light. **Step 2: Outlining the Facial Features** Divide the face into three parts vertically using two intersecting lines. The first line should run through the center of the face, while the second one divides the lower half equally. Horizontally, mark another line about halfway down the head to indicate where the eyes will go. **Step 3: Adding Eyes and Nose** On the horizontal line, place two tiny dots close together for the eyes. Just above them, add two smaller curved marks for eyebrows. For the nose, draw a short vertical line between the eyes with slight curves at both ends. **Step 4: Defining Mouth & Cheeks** Below the nose, draw a slightly curved line for the mouth. Above it, etch out two tiny crescent moons for cheeks. Note that unlike humans, Smurfs have their mouths usually upturned indicating their cheerful nature. **Step 5: Creating the Hat** Erase any overlapping lines around the top part of the head and replace them with a floppy blue hat. It consists of a band going across the forehead, followed by a cylindrical cap, and finally a brim at the top. Don't forget the red pom-pom! **Step 6: Designing the Body** Now let's move onto the body. Start by defining the arms which are typically drawn straight down with hands resting on hips or holding something. Then, work on the legs which are usually depicted as stubby sticks coming out from under the coat. **Step 7: Detailing Clothes** Papa Smurf wears a long white robe tied at the waist with a red belt. Use gentle strokes to depict folds and creases in the fabric. Also, remember to include his traditional glasses hanging from the rope tied around his waist. **Step 8: Refining Lines & Erasing Guidelines** Go over all your pencil strokes ensuring they're clean and confident. Once satisfied, gently erase all construction lines leaving only the final image behind. If needed, darken certain areas for emphasis. **Step 9: Coloring Your Masterpiece** Grab some coloring tools (markers, colored pencils, watercolors) and fill in the appropriate colors. Smurfs are primarily blue, but feel free to get creative with skin tones, hair color, clothing shades etc., keeping true to the original design. **Conclusion:** Congratulations! You've successfully drawn a Smurf! Practice makes perfect, so don't worry if your first few attempts aren't flawless. With time, patience, and dedication, you'll master this art form and create stunning pieces worthy of admiration. Happy drawing! Washington, D.C. was planned before it was built. Unlike most cities in the United States, D.C. has many roundabouts or traffic circles. The city was supposed to have long and wide avenues, and many open spaces for monuments and parks. " [PLA raises the cup to his lips to pretend to drink] Title: How to Delete Items on Your Computer That You Don't Want to Part With Introduction: Deleting files from your computer can be a challenging task when you are dealing with sentimental attachments or important data. However, there are methods to help you declutter your digital space without completely losing those precious memories or vital documents. This guide will walk you through the process of deleting items on your computer while ensuring that you have taken the necessary precautions to preserve what matters most. **Step 1: Identify Unnecessary Files and Folders** Before starting the deletion process, identify the files and folders you no longer need. These could include duplicate photos, old downloads, temporary internet files, and applications that are no longer used. By doing this, you reduce the risk of accidentally deleting something valuable. Key Tip: Use tools like Disk Cleanup (Windows) or About This Mac > Storage > Manage (Mac) to quickly scan and remove unnecessary system files. **Step 2: Back Up Important Data** For files you wish to keep but do not regularly access, consider backing them up using an external storage device such as USB drives, hard disks, or cloud-based services like Google Drive, Dropbox, etc. Regular backups ensure that even if you delete a file by mistake, you still have access to its latest version. Guideline: Make sure to encrypt sensitive data before storing it externally. Also, periodically check your backup destinations for any corrupted or outdated files. **Step 3: Create Archive Copies** If you find yourself unable to permanently delete large collections of data due to emotional attachment, create archive copies instead. Compress these into .zip or .rar formats and store them in designated folders labeled 'Archived'. This way, they won't take up much space yet remain accessible whenever needed. Key Tip: On Windows, right click on a folder -> Send To -> Compressed (zipped) Folder. For Mac users, Control-click on a folder -> Compress. **Step 4: Empty Recycle Bin / Trash Can Periodically** Once you've moved unwanted files into the recycle bin or trash can, make it a habit to empty them regularly. Both operating systems automatically clear their respective waste bins after a certain period, but manually doing so ensures prompt removal of deleted files. Guideline: Set reminders every week or month to perform this task until it becomes routine. **Step 5: Permanently Shred Sensitive Documents** When disposing of highly confidential information, simply moving it to the recycling bin isn't enough. Utilize specialized software designed for securely wiping data beyond recovery, such as Eraser (for Windows) or Secure Delete (for Mac). Key Tip: Always remember to shred only those files which aren't required anymore. Conclusion: Following these steps allows you to maintain control over your digital clutter while preserving essential data. Remember, regular maintenance is crucial in keeping your computer organized and efficient. Happy cleaning! Title: How to Enable Vibration Mode for Alarms on a Timex Expedition W-214 Wristwatch The Timex Expedition W-214 wristwatch is a reliable and durable timepiece suitable for various outdoor activities. This watch features an Indiglo light-up dial, multiple time zones, countdown timer, stopwatch, and daily alarms with vibration mode. If you're wondering how to enable vibration mode for alarms on your Timex Expedition W-214, this comprehensive guide will walk you through the process step by step. **Step 1: Understanding the Watch Layout** Before diving into enabling vibration mode, familiarize yourself with the watch layout. The Timex Expedition W-214 has several buttons that perform specific functions: * Top right button (SET): Used for setting alarms, timers, and other options. * Bottom right button (MODE): Switches between different modes such as time, alarm, timer, etc. * Top left button (LIGHT): Activates the Indiglo nightlight feature. * Bottom left button (START/SPLIT): Operates the stopwatch or splits lap times during workouts. **Step 2: Accessing the Alarm Menu** Press the bottom right button (MODE) until the display shows the ALARM icon. Then press the top right button (SET) to enter the alarm menu. Here, you can set up to three separate alarms using the hour and minute buttons. Key Tip: To cycle through the available alarms, keep pressing the MODE button. Each alarm slot is labeled A, B, and C. **Step 3: Setting the Desired Alarm Time** Once you have selected an alarm slot, use the hour and minute buttons to adjust the time according to your preference. After setting the time, press the SET button again to save your changes. **Step 4: Enabling Vibration Mode** Now comes the main part - enabling vibration mode for the alarm. With the alarm time still displayed, press and hold the START/SPLIT button until the watch beeps and displays 'VIBE.' Once you see 'VIBE,' release the button knowing that vibration mode has been enabled successfully. Guideline: When the alarm goes off, the watch will now gently vibrate instead of producing sound, making it perfect for situations where discretion is required. **Step 5: Testing Your Settings** To ensure everything works correctly, wait until the alarm should go off based on your settings. Upon activation, the watch will either emit a soft vibration or a tone depending on whether or not vibration was activated. Additional Tips: * Remember that enabling both vibration and audible tones simultaneously drains battery life faster than usual. Disable one if preserving power is essential. * You cannot enable vibration mode while the countdown timer is running; first, turn off the timer before proceeding. By following these steps carefully, you can effortlessly activate vibration mode for any alarm on your Timex Expedition W-214 wristwatch. Enjoy exploring its full potential! Let's learn about distances and how to solve problems involving them. Imagine you are a superhero bird, like a peacock, and you see a sneaky snake slithering towards its hole at the base of a tall tree. The tree is 12 meters high, and you notice the snake is approaching from a point that is three times the height of the tree away. That means the snake is 12m x 3 = 36 meters away from the tree's base. Now, you and the snake start moving toward each other at the same speed. You fly in a straight line while the snake keeps crawling along the ground. When do you two meet, or more specifically, at what distance from the tree's base does this encounter happen? To answer this question, let's follow these steps together! 1. **Draw a picture**: It always helps to visualize the situation with a drawing. Picture the tree, the snake's initial position, and your starting spot. Label the important points and measurements to keep track of everything easily. In our case, we have the tree (T), the snake's original location (S), the point where you and the snake meet (M), and the tree's base (B). We know the heights of T and S relative to B, and the horizontal distance between S and T. ![Peacock_Snake Diagram](https://i.imgur.com/4pqW58Q.png) 2. **Use similar triangles**: Since both the tree and the hypothetical line connecting S and T are twelve meters tall, and the snake starts at a point that is three times farther than the tree's height, we actually have two similar triangles here-Triangle STM and Triangle BTM. Similar triangles share corresponding angles and side ratios. So, using this relationship, we can say that BM divided by SM equals the ratio of their sides, which is 1 (tree height) : x (distance). This gives us an easy proportion to work with: BM / SM = 12m / (12m + z) Where z represents the distance you travel before catching up with the snake. 3. **Set up the equation**: Now, remember that BM equals the horizontal distance between the snake and the tree, so it's simply 36 meters. And when you finally reach the snake, the combined distance traveled by both you and the snake should equal the distance between the snake and the tree. Mathematically speaking: SM + z = 36m 4. **Combine the information**: By combining these statements, we get: 36m / (36m - z) = 12m / (12m + z) Cross-multiplying leads to the following quadratic equation: 12(36) = 12z + z^2 Simplifying further results in: z^2 + 12z - 432 = 0 5. **Solve the equation**: Although solving quadratics isn't typically covered in grade school, you can still appreciate that there's a specific method to crack this nut open. Using factoring techniques or the quadratic formula, we can determine that one possible value for z is 12 meters, making the second root negative and thus irrelevant since distances cannot be negative. So, hooray! After flying for just 12 meters, you'll intercept the sneaky snake trying to hide near its hole. Keep practicing these thinking skills to become a better problem solver! Creativity is a central question in economics, where it is known as ingenuity (the ability to come up with new ideas) or individual capital - capacities that individuals have, that do not arise from simple imitation of what is known already. This is separate from the instructional capital that might try to capture some of that in a patent or training system that helps others do what the individual leader or founder of the system can do. In urban economics there are various ways to measure creativity - the Bohemian Index and Gay Index are two attempts to do this accurately and predict the economic growth of cities based on creativity. Title: How to Download a Resume from the Indeed App Introduction: Indeed is one of the most popular job search platforms that allows users to upload their resumes for potential employers to view. The Indeed app also enables you to access your resume on-the-go. However, downloading your resume directly from the Indeed app isn't as straightforward as you might think. This tutorial will guide you through the process with clear, detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. Step 1: Open the Indeed App Start by opening the Indeed app on your mobile device. If you haven't already signed in, tap 'Sign in' at the top right corner and enter your credentials. Once logged in, proceed to the home screen. Key Tip: Make sure you have a stable internet connection throughout this entire process to avoid any issues or interruptions. Step 2: Access Your Account Settings Tap on the three horizontal lines (hamburger menu) located at the upper left corner of the home screen. Scroll down until you find 'Account settings,' then tap on it. This action takes you to the account management section where you can modify various aspects related to your profile. Guideline: Remember that changing certain details like contact information should be done carefully, ensuring all data provided remains accurate and up-to-date. Step 3: Navigate to Your Resume Under the 'Personal Info & Privacy' category within 'Account settings', select 'Resume'. Here, you can see an overview of your resume including its visibility status and last updated date. Step 4: Preview Your Resume Before downloading, take some time to preview your resume. Check if there are necessary edits or updates needed. To do so, simply tap 'Preview'. Review everything thoroughly - from formatting to content. Key Tip: Regularly updating your resume keeps it fresh and relevant, increasing chances of being noticed by recruiters. Step 5: Save Locally on Your Device Unfortunately, the Indeed app doesn't offer a direct 'Download' button for resumes. But don't worry! There's a workaround using the built-in share function. After reviewing your resume, go back to the previous page, hit the 'Share' icon usually represented by a box with an arrow pointing outwards. Choose the 'Save to Files' option which allows you to save the PDF version of your resume locally onto your device storage or cloud service such as iCloud Drive, Google Drive, Dropbox etc. Step 6: Confirm Successful Download After selecting 'Save to Files', choose where you want to store your resume (for example, in the 'Documents' folder). Then click 'Save.' A notification confirming successful saving appears shortly after. Now, you can find and open your resume anytime without needing internet access. Conclusion: While downloading a resume directly from the Indeed app may seem challenging initially, following these six simple yet detailed steps makes it easy. Always remember to keep your resume current and make full use of available features for maximum exposure to potential employers. Happy job hunting! Hey Nina we haven't finished snacks yet. Title: Get Your Teeth White Quickly Introduction: White teeth can significantly improve your smile and boost your confidence. While there are many products and procedures available that promise quick results, some methods may be harsh or expensive. This guide will provide you with safe, effective, and affordable ways to get your teeth white quickly. Remember, maintaining good oral hygiene is essential for keeping your teeth healthy and bright. Section 1: Understanding Tooth Discoloration Before diving into the whitening process, it's crucial to understand what causes tooth discoloration. There are two main types: extrinsic and intrinsic stains. Extrinsic stains occur on the surface of the teeth due to factors like food, drinks, tobacco, and poor dental hygiene. Intrinsic stains form inside the tooth, often as a result of aging, genetics, trauma, or certain medications. Different whitening techniques work better for specific types of stains, so identifying the cause of your discoloration will help determine the best approach. Section 2: Preparing for Teeth Whitening 1. Consult your dentist: Before starting any whitening treatment, consult your dentist to ensure it's safe and suitable for your teeth. They can also recommend professional treatments if necessary. 2. Assess your oral health: Make sure your gums and teeth are healthy before beginning a whitening regimen. Treat cavities, gum disease, or other issues first to prevent sensitivity or pain during the whitening process. 3. Set realistic expectations: Keep in mind that individual results vary, and severely discolored teeth might require more than one session or professional intervention. TINY TIM. There was once a man who did not like Christmas. Title: How to Control Your Emotions =================================== Controlling your emotions is an important life skill that can help you navigate difficult situations, maintain healthy relationships, and improve your overall well-being. This tutorial will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to control your emotions effectively. ### Step 1: Identify Your Emotions The first step in controlling your emotions is to identify them accurately. Pay attention to physical sensations, thoughts, and behaviors associated with different emotions. For example, anxiety may be accompanied by rapid heartbeat, worry, and avoidance behavior. By recognizing what you're feeling, you take the initial step towards managing those feelings. Key Tip: Remember that all emotions are valid and natural; they serve as signals about our experiences and needs. Aim to understand rather than judge or suppress them. ### Step 2: Understand Triggers Emotional responses often arise from specific triggers. These could be external events (like criticism) or internal thoughts (such as self-doubt). Once you know your triggers, you can anticipate emotional reactions and prepare strategies to respond constructively instead of reacting impulsively. Guideline: Reflect on past incidents where strong emotions were present. What led up to these moments? Are there common themes? Recognize patterns to predict future occurrences. ### Step 3: Practice Mindfulness Mindfulness involves focusing on the present moment without judgment. When practiced regularly, mindfulness can increase self-awareness, reduce stress, and enhance emotion regulation. Techniques include deep breathing, progressive muscle relaxation, body scans, and meditation. Key Tips: Start small - even just five minutes per day can make a difference. Consistency is crucial; make mindfulness part of your daily routine. ### Step 4: Challenge Negative Thoughts Negative thought patterns can exacerbate negative emotions. Learning to challenge these thoughts can lead to more balanced perspectives and improved mood. Ask yourself if your thoughts are factual, fair, helpful, or kind. If not, try reframing them in a positive light. Guideline: Use the 'ABCDE' method: Acknowledge the activating event, Believe your automatic thought, Consider alternative beliefs, Decide on a new response, and Evaluate the result. ### Step 5: Develop Healthy Coping Mechanisms Relying on unhealthy coping mechanisms (like substance abuse or overeating) during times of emotional distress can worsen problems in the long run. Instead, cultivate positive habits such as exercise, journaling, art therapy, or talking to trusted friends or professionals. Key Tip: Experiment with various methods to discover which ones work best for you. Having multiple options ensures versatility in dealing with diverse emotional states. ### Step 6: Seek Professional Help If despite consistent effort, you find it challenging to manage your emotions, consider seeking professional assistance. Therapists and counselors specialize in teaching evidence-based techniques to regulate emotions effectively. Guidelines: Look for licensed practitioners who have experience working with individuals facing similar issues. Don't hesitate to ask questions before committing to a course of treatment. In conclusion, mastering emotion regulation requires patience, practice, and persistence. By following these steps, however, you significantly enhance your ability to control your emotions, leading to enhanced mental health and general well-being. First trouble it's not a school day. Title: How to Check Your PHP Version: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Knowing your server's PHP version is crucial when developing or maintaining PHP-based web applications. This guide will walk you through various methods to check your PHP version with clear explanations, helpful tips, and best practices. Let's get started! Method 1: Using phpinfo() Function (Recommended) Step 1: Create a new file in your website root directory or any accessible location within your web environment. Name this file 'phpversion.php'. The filename does not have strict requirements but should be descriptive enough to indicate its purpose. Key tip: Choose an appropriate name that reflects the content and usage of the file, making it easy to manage and remove once no longer needed. Step 2: Open the newly created file ('phpversion.php') using a text editor such as Notepad, Sublime Text, Visual Studio Code, or Atom. Paste the following code snippet into the file: ```php <?php phpinfo(); ?> ``` Explanation: By calling the `phpinfo()` function, we tell PHP to output extensive information about itself, including the active version, installed modules, configuration settings, and more. It serves as a handy diagnostic tool for developers working with PHP environments. Guideline: Be cautious about exposing sensitive data like database credentials by limiting access to the generated page. Step 3: Save changes made to the file and upload it to your server via FTP, SFTP, SCP, or any preferred method used to transfer files between local machines and servers. Key tip: Double-check if the file has been successfully transferred without corruption before proceeding further. Step 4: Access the file from your browser by typing the URL corresponding to the file path. For instance, if you placed the file inside the public_html folder, browse to "[yourdomain.com](http://yourdomain.com)/phpversion.php". You can also use IP addresses instead of domain names if necessary. Expected result: Upon loading, you should see a comprehensive report detailing the PHP installation, including the current version number at the top of the page. Step 5: Once you have noted down the required information, remember to delete or securely store the 'phpversion.php' file since leaving it publicly available might pose security risks. Method 2: Through Command Line Interface (CLI) Step 1: Log in to your server via SSH using Terminal (MacOS & Linux), PowerShell (Windows), or other terminal emulators. Use valid login credentials - typically username and password or SSH keys. Step 2: Type the command below and press Enter to display the PHP version running on CLI: ```bash php -v ``` Explanation: Execution of this simple command line instruction invokes the PHP binary followed by the lowercase letter v flag indicating the request for version details. As a response, the system outputs the currently installed and enabled PHP version along with some additional information. Key tip: Keep track of both webserver and CLI PHP versions as they may differ due to separate configurations and update schedules. Conclusion: In conclusion, checking your PHP version involves either generating a dynamic report containing relevant PHP information through the `phpinfo()` function or querying the PHP executable directly from the command line interface. Both approaches serve their purposes depending on specific needs while emphasizing the importance of understanding one's development environment. Now we're going to go for a ride. The government of the UK triggered "Article 50" of the Treaty of European Union (the Treaty of Lisbon) on 29 March 2017. This began negotiations with fellow members of the EU on the terms of exit. The timetable for these negotiations is two years, which meant that the UK would remain a member of the EU until at least March 2019. However this deadline was later extended to October 31st 2019 at the request of the British Government. The United Kingdom left the European Union on the 31 of January 2020 at 23:00 (Greenwich Mean Time). Following the UK left from the European Union on January 31, the transition period expired on December 31, 2020 and ended on January 1, 2021. An exponent is a number that tells how many times the base number is used as a factor. For example, 43 indicates that the base number 4 is used as a factor 3 times. To determine the value of 43, multiply 4*4*4 which would give the result 64. Cubes indicate that the exponent has a value of three. The term cube comes from the geometrical shape that has the same width and length and height. To find the volume of a cube you would multiply the width times the length times the height. Exponents are written as a superscript number (e.g. 43) or preceded by the caret (^) symbol (e.g. 4^3). Some facts about exponents: - Zero cubed is zero (e.g. 03 = 0) - One cubed is one (e.g. 13 = 1) Silver is not a large danger to humans. Silver compounds are toxic. They make the skin turn blue. Some can be carcinogens. Colloidal silver, a common homeopathic remedy, is not toxic in normal amounts, but it does not do much. Kauai (Kaua'i in Hawaiian) is the second oldest (after Ni'ihau) and fourth largest of the main Hawaiian Islands, in the United States. Known also as the "Garden Isle", Kaua'i lies 73 miles (117 kilometers) across the Kauai Channel, northwest of Honolulu on Oahu. It is of volcanic origin. The highest point on the island is Kawaikini. It is located above sea level. The wettest spot on Earth, with average rainfall of a year, is just east of Mount Waialeale. The high yearly rainfall has eroded deep valleys and canyons in the central mountain. The waterfalls that have been created by erosion in canyons are now popular tourist spots. "Yes, sir." Hiring a lawyer on contingency fee can be an affordable way to pursue legal action when you're unable to pay upfront costs. This payment arrangement allows you to postpone payment until your case is resolved, at which point your attorney will receive a percentage of any settlement or award you receive. Here's a comprehensive guide on hiring a lawyer on contingency fee: ### Step 1: Determine if Your Case Qualifies for Contingency Fees Not all cases are suitable for contingency fees. Generally, personal injury, employment discrimination, medical malpractice, and debt collection cases qualify. However, criminal defense, family law, and estate planning matters usually do not. Consult with potential attorneys to see if they accept such arrangements for your type of case. Key tip: Be aware that some lawyers may charge higher contingency fees (up to 40%) if a lawsuit has been filed compared to pre-litigation cases (around 33%). Also, state laws regulate maximum contingency fee percentages, so check local regulations. ### Step 2: Research Potential Attorneys Look for experienced lawyers who specialize in your area of need. Check their credentials, client reviews, success rates, and disciplinary records via bar association websites or third-party review platforms like Martindale-Hubbell, Avvo, or Justia. Narrow down your list based on these factors. Guideline: Aim for attorneys with at least five years of experience and positive client testimonials. Make sure they have no history of misconduct or malpractice claims. ### Step 3: Schedule Initial Consultations Most lawyers offer free initial consultations. Prepare by gathering relevant documents, writing down questions, and practicing your summary of events. During the meeting, discuss your goals, expectations, and concerns. Ask about their experience, strategy, communication style, timeline, and estimated costs. Key tip: Bring a friend or relative along for support and note-taking during meetings. Trust your instincts - if you don't feel comfortable with an attorney, consider finding another one. ### Step 4: Understand Contingency Fee Agreements Review and negotiate the terms of the contingency fee agreement before signing. It should include: * The percentage of the recovery the lawyer will take as their fee * Whether expenses (like filing fees, expert witness costs) are deducted from the total recovery or paid separately * Who pays court costs if you lose the case * How often progress reports will be provided * What happens if the case settles without trial * Any circumstances under which the attorney could withdraw from representation Guideline: Never sign anything hastily; take time to read and understand every detail. Don't hesitate to ask questions or seek clarification. ### Step 5: Maintain Open Communication Once hired, maintain regular contact with your lawyer. Share updates, provide requested information promptly, and express any concerns immediately. Remember, your involvement contributes significantly to achieving a favorable outcome. Key tip: Keep track of important dates, deadlines, and appointments related to your case. Regularly review bills and invoices for accuracy. By following this tutorial, you increase chances of successfully hiring a lawyer on contingency fee basis while ensuring transparency, control, and affordability throughout the process. This is the case for Our Lady of Sorrows Church, Poprad, in Slovakia, for example. In Slovak language, this church is called "cathedral". It is part of the Roman Catholic Diocese of Spi . This diocese was re-organised several times. Currently, the cathedral of the diocese is St. Martin's Cathedral (Spi sk Kapitula). Churches that formerly were a diocesan seat may be called concathedral, co-cathedral, or pro-cathedral. They had many colors of crayons, but they both loved orange the most. They always wanted to use the orange crayon for their pictures. One day, they found only one orange crayon in their box. They both reached for it at the same time. They pulled and tugged, but neither of them would let go. "Give me the orange crayon!" Tim said. "I saw it first!" "No, I did!" Sam said. "It's mine!" They started to yell and fight over the orange crayon. They did not seem like friends anymore. Their teacher saw them and came over. She was not happy. She took the orange crayon from them and said, "You two need to learn to share. If you can't share, you can't play with the crayons at all." She put the orange crayon away and gave them each a different color. Tim got a blue crayon and Sam got a green crayon. They looked at their crayons and felt sad. They missed the orange crayon. They looked at each other and felt sorry. They realized they had been silly and mean. They decided to say sorry and make up. "I'm sorry, Sam," Tim said. "You can have the orange crayon next time." "I'm sorry, Tim," Sam said. "You can have it now. I like green too." They hugged and smiled. They started to draw with their crayons. They made a picture of a sun with the orange crayon and a grass with the green crayon. They added a sky with the blue crayon. They wrote their names on the picture and gave it to their teacher. Their teacher was proud of them. She said, "That's a beautiful picture. And you know what? You two are beautiful friends. You learned to share and be kind. That's more important than any color." Much of what we understand about prehistoric sharks comes from the study of their fossils. While sharks have skeletons made of soft cartilage that can fall apart before fossilizing, their teeth are harder and easily fossilized. Prehistoric sharks, like their modern descendants, would grow and shed many thousands of teeth over their lifetime. For this reason shark teeth are one of the most common fossils. he's over here on the floor. Can you bang your head on the shower? Today, let's talk about something exciting called the "QR Decomposition." You might be wondering, "What on earth is that?" Well, it's just a fancy name for breaking down a rectangular array (which mathematicians call a "matrix") into two special parts. These parts will make it easier for us to understand certain properties of the original matrix. So, grab some paper and pencils, and let's dive in! Imagine you have a big box full of toys arranged in neat rows and columns - like a mini toy store! Each row could represent different types of toys, while each column tells you which toys were placed next to one another. This arrangement helps you quickly find your favorite cars, dolls, or action figures without messing up the whole collection. Now, our goal is to rearrange these toys so that all the cars appear first in consecutive order, followed by the dolls, and finally the action figures. To do this, we need to perform two steps: 1. First, create smaller groups within the existing boxes where each group contains similar toys (cars together, dolls together, etc.). We will refer to these groups as "Q". Note that the number of total boxes remains unchanged. 2. Secondly, arrange the items inside each new box (group) in ascending order based on their type - starting from cars, then dolls, and ending with action figures. Let's denote these ordered arrangements as "R". So now, our initial toy configuration ($A$) has been broken down into the product of $Q$ (our small groups) and $R$ (the internal ordering of those groups): $A = QR$. And voila! The magic of QR Decomposition! In summary, QR Decomposition takes a complicated matrix and splits it into two simpler arrays, making further mathematical analysis more manageable. While I've illustrated this concept using toys, remember that mathematicians apply this technique to numbers instead of playthings. It may seem abstract now, but who knows? Maybe someday you too will appreciate the beauty of math through fun tools like QR Decompositions! [Passing a truck that was pulling out.] Would you like to get one of your books now? Your kids can work in small groups to find how movement chanes when there is more - and less - friction. On 18 April 1985 the "Falkland Islands Constitution Order 1985" came into force which increased the number of elected members of the Legislative Council to eight. It was agreed on by the UK Government and the Falkland Islands Government. The new constitution provided for finance, the public service, the administration of justice, and a Complaints Commissioner. Frank said sadly, "My fountain is broken and I can't fix it." But Sam had a plan! He said, "If you come with me I might know someone who can help you fix your fountain." So Frank and Sam went out into the woods, looking for help. And soon they found Snail Bob! Title: How to Delete an App from Your Facebook Profile Facebook allows you to connect with various apps and websites to enhance your social media experience. However, if you no longer want to use a particular app or have concerns about your privacy, you can delete it from your Facebook profile. Here's a comprehensive guide on how to do this: 1. Log into your Facebook account. To start the process, ensure you are logged into your Facebook account where the app is installed. This will give you access to all the necessary settings and options required to remove the app. 2. Navigate to Settings & Privacy. Click on the arrow pointing downwards at the top right corner of your screen next to the question mark icon. From the dropdown menu that appears, select 'Settings & Privacy', then click on 'Settings'. 3. Access Apps and Websites. On the left side of the page under the 'Security and Login' section, click on 'Apps and Websites'. This action opens up another window displaying all the applications connected to your Facebook account. 4. Locate the App You Want to Remove. Scroll through the list until you find the application you wish to delete. The list includes details such as the name of the app, the date when you added it, and whether it has access to your public profile, friend list, email address, etc. 5. Edit App Settings. Once you've found the app, click on the pencil-like edit icon located at the end of its row. By doing so, you'll be directed to a new page containing specific information and settings related to that app. 6. Remove App. At the bottom of the edited app settings page, there should be an option labeled 'Remove App'. Click on this button. A confirmation box may appear asking if you're sure you want to proceed; confirm by clicking 'Remove'. Remember, deleting an app does not automatically mean it loses any data associated with it unless explicitly stated otherwise within the app's terms or policies. 7. Review Removed Apps. After successfully removing the app, go back to the main 'Apps and Websites' settings page (Step 3). Under the header 'Removed', you should see the deleted app listed along with others previously removed. If you accidentally removed an app or changed your mind later, you could restore it here without having to reauthorize full permissions again. Key Tips and Guidelines: - Regularly review connected apps - Make it a habit to check the apps linked to your Facebook profile regularly. Unused or unnecessary ones pose potential security risks. - Be cautious about what info you share - Before connecting an app to your Facebook profile, understand what kind of data it collects and why. Only authorize those aligning with your comfort level. - Use Facebook's login approval feature - Enabling this feature adds an extra layer of protection against unauthorized logins using saved credentials. - Report suspicious apps - If you come across an app behaving suspiciously or infringing upon user rights, report it immediately via the 'Report Problem' link at the bottom of every Facebook page. The Colorado beetle is famous for its ability to resist pesticides. Over the last 50 years it has become resistant to 52 chemical compounds used in insecticides, including cyanide. This is natural selection speeded up by the artificial conditions. However, not every population is resistant to every chemical. The populations only become resistant to chemicals used in their area. [Lin is holding onto the ball and turns away from MOT] [opens the box of crayons for NEA to see] - slide 1 of 2 How Simple Machines Work What is a simple machine and how do they work? I'm so glad you asked! Machines make work easier by changing the size of force, direction of force, or distance the force acts on. Lifting a car with a flat tire and loosening the lugnuts can be accomplished by a single person thanks to simple machines. The jack and lug wrench are simple machines that alter the force needed to change the tire. - slide 2 of 2 Six Simple Machines Simple machines are basic devices used to alter the force needed to accomplish a task. There are six types of simple machines. The first type of simple machine is the lever. A lever is a rigid bar that rotates on the fixed point of a fulcrum and changes the distance or size of a force. There are three classes of levers. A first class lever has an input force and output force on either side of the fulcrum. This causes the output to move in the opposite direction of the the input force. An example of a first class lever is a see-saw. A second class lever has an output force between the input force and fulcrum. This changes the distance of the force. A wheelbarrow is a second class lever. The third class lever has the input force between the output and fulcrum. A broom is a third class lever. Wheel and Axle The wheel and axle make work easier by changing the distance the force acts on. A wheel and axle consists of two disks or cylinders with different radiuses. Examples are a steering wheel and shaft, a car wheel and axle, and a screwdriver. An inclined plane is a slanted surface on which a force can move an object to a different elevation. Why do gentler slopes and ramps require less energy to move a load on? Because the input force required to travel the greater distance of a slope is changed to the smaller distance of the output force - the upward motion. A wedge is a device made of two back to back inclined planes and is used to split objects. When a wedge is driven into a log, the size of the input force at the wider top of the wedge is changed to greater output force at the narrower point forcing the wedge through the wood. Knife blades are an example of a wedge. A screw is an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder. Screws with threads closer together require less force to turn because the length of the inclined plane is longer. Nuts and bolts are screws. A nut is a screw with the threads on the inside. The last type of simple machine is the pulley. A pulley consists of a rope that fits into a groove in a wheel. A pulley makes work easier by changing the direction or direction and size of the force. There are three types of pulleys. They are the fixed pulley, moveable pulley and pulley system. A moveable pulley is fixed to the object being moved instead of a fixed location. Moveable pulleys multiply the input force needed to lift a heavy object thus reducing the force needed to lift heavy objects. Moveable pulleys are used to move ship sails and window washer platforms. Pulley systems combine fixed and moveable pulleys to create large mechanical advantages. A crane uses pulley systems to lift enormous loads like locomotives. - Michael Wysession, David Frank, Sophia Yancopoulos. Physical Science Concepts in Action. p.417 - 435. New Jersey: Prentice Hall, 2004. Once there was a little girl who was curious. Whenever she saw something new, she wanted to try it. One day, she found a furry thing on the floor. She thought it was very interesting. She picked it up and said to herself, What is this? Just then, her mom came up to her. She saw the small, furry object and said, That s a match. The little girl looked confused. What does it do? she asked. Well, it s used to make a fire in a sink , her mom replied. The little girl thought for a moment before she said, Oh. Like when we cook food? Her mom smiled. Yes, exactly. She took the match from the little one and lit it with her thumb. See, now it will help us cook. The little girl liked playing with the furry match. She watched as her mom used it to light the fire in the sink. She clapped happily, excited to learn something new. What did the book say that penguins can't fly? Bang The Drum Rationale: This lesson will help children identify /b/, the phoneme represented by B. Students will learn to recognize /b/ in spoken words by learning a meaningful representation (banging the drum) and the letter symbol B, practice--finding /b/ in words, and apply phoneme awareness with /b/ in phonetic cue reading by distinguishing rhyming words beginning letters. chart with "Bob bought blue birds" drawing paper and crayons Book Bouncing on the Bed( Orchard Book 1999) word cards with -bold, flat, meet, beat, teeth, brush, bang, sang assessment worksheet (url below) 1. Say, "Our written language is a secret code. The tricky part is learning what stand the mouth moves, when we say words. Today we are going to work spotting the mouth move /b/. We spell /b/ with letter B. /b/ sounds like what a drum makes when you bang it. 2. Let's pretend to bang the drum,/b//b/b/.Making the sound of the drum. Notice where your lips come together, (touch one another). When we say /b/, we blow air between our lips. 3. Let me show you how to find /b/ in the word babble. I am going to stretch babble out in supper slow motion and listen for my drum sound. b--aaaa--bbbb--bbbb--llleeeee. There it was I felt my lips come together and blow air. I can hear the drum sound in babble. 4. Let's try a tongue tickler (on chart). "Bob bought blue birds." Everybody say it three times together. Now say it again, and this time, stretch the /b/ at the beginning of the words. "Bbbbbob bbbought bbblue bbbbirds." Try it again, and this time break the word: /B/ ob /b/ ought /b/ lue /b/irds. 5. Have students take out primary paper and pencil. We use letter B to spell /b/. Capital B looks like two bubbles. Let us write the lowercase b. Start at the rooftop, make a straight line down and bbbb bounce back up and around. I want to see everybody's b. After I put a smiley face on it, I want you to make nine more just like it. 6. All the stress and strain produced by moving plates builds up in the Earth's rocky crust until it simply can't take it any more. All at once, CRACK!, the rock breaks and the two rocky blocks move in opposite directions along a more or less planar fracture surface called a fault. The sudden movement generates an earthquake at a point called the focus. The energy from the earthquake spreads out as seismic waves in all directions. The epicenter of the earthquake is the location where seismic waves reach the surface directly above the focus. Now, consider this: if we hold the foot wall stationary, gravity will normally want to pull the hanging wall down, right? Faults that move the way you would expect gravity to move them normally are called normal faults! Not so hard, is it? Take a look where the fault has ruptured the Earth surface. Notice that movement along the fault has produced an elongate cliff? That fault-generated cliff is called a fault scarp. Can you see the foot-shaped foot wall and the hanging wall resting or hanging above it? Think about this: if we hold the foot wall stationary, where would the hanging wall go if we reversed gravity? The hanging wall will slide upwards, right? When movement along a fault is the reverse of what you would expect with normal gravity we call them reverse faults! The rocky blocks on either side of strike-slip faults, on the other hand, scrape along side-by-side. You can see in the illustration that the movement is horizontal and the rock layers beneath the surface haven't been moved up or down on either side of the fault. Take a look where the fault has ruptured the Earth surface. Notice that pure strike-slip faults do not produce fault scarps. There are other tell-tale changes in the landscape that signal strike-slip faulting. As you might guess, where the two massive blocks on either side of a strike-slip fault grind against each other, rock is weakened. Streams flowing across strike-slip faults are often diverted to flow along this weakened zone. Most characters, however, are made by combining pictures, using one to give a general meaning, and the other to represent a sound. For instance, " m " (mom) is made by adding (n , human female) to (m , horse). The "ma" part is only there to represent the sound. Title: How to Perform Divide and Conquer on the Iris Dataset The Iris dataset is one of the most famous datasets used in machine learning and statistics. It consists of 150 samples from three species of iris (Iris setosa, Iris versicolor, and Iris virginica). Each sample has four features or attributes: sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width. This tutorial will guide you through performing divide-and-conquer on the Iris dataset using Python and its libraries like NumPy, pandas, matplotlib, and scikit-learn. By following this tutorial, you'll gain insights into data exploration, visualization, feature scaling, model training, evaluation, and prediction. **Step 1:** Import Necessary Libraries Key tip: Make sure that your environment has all necessary packages installed before running the code below. If any package isn't available, install them with pip or conda. ```python import numpy as np import pandas as pd import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from sklearn import datasets from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler from sklearn.neighbors import KNeighborsClassifier from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, classification_report, confusion_matrix ``` Explanation: The above lines load essential modules needed for our task - managing arrays (NumPy), working with structured data (pandas), plotting charts (matplotlib), loading and handling datasets (scikit-learn), and statistical analysis & modeling (scikit-learn). **Step 2:** Load the Iris Dataset An' you will have a marble too. Teaching Your Children Pedestrian Safety Walking on the inside of the sidewalk at all times. Walking close to the outside edge of the sidewalk (curb) is very dangerous for children. They can be hit by reckless drivers, and they can dart out into the street faster than an adult can react. Teach children that every sidewalk has two sides: a safe inside area, close to the houses and/or the grass; and a dangerous outside area, close to the road. KID BRAKES: Stopping before the edge of the sidewalk at all times. Children can't see what's going on around them as well as adults can because they are smaller. They must come to a complete stop before they can be expected to look for traffic. Children can also be easily distracted by a dog, a friend or a noise and forget to stop altogether. Teach children to treat their feet as "KID BRAKES" and the edge of the sidewalk as a barrier where they must always stop. KID BRAKES: Stopping at driveways, alleys and areas without curbs. Children are not drivers - they may not know that there are many different kinds of access routes. For the same reason, children are also not aware that a driver using these routes may not be able to see everything around them. Teach children when and how to make their own barriers in places other than sidewalks. Point out special areas such as alleys and driveways. Explain how drivers in these areas sometimes have trouble seeing around them and why it is important for children to be on the alert. Stopping at areas without curbs Find a place that has no curbs (a new subdivision, for example) Standing well off the road, ask the child to look for oncoming traffic. If he/she can't see down the road, take a small step forward. The idea is to be a safe distance from passing cars but not so far that the child's view is blocked by trees, etc. Repeat in different places until the child understands how to create a "barrier" in places where there is no curb. Explain to the child that this barrier is where he/she must always STOP and apply KID BRAKES. Teach the child to look for cars that may be pulling in or pulling out and explain how the driver's view in these situations is sometimes blocked. Making "Stop, Look and Listen" a habit. Children have short attention spans and are easily distracted. Safety must become a habit while they are still young to make up for these natural tendencies. Teach children by example: be an adult who practices traffic safety in an obvious way. Repeat "Stop, Look and Listen" often. Challenge children to learn the habit and reward them when they do. Back to top Crossing the street in the middle of the block. Children will often cross in the middle of the block rather then walk to a corner because they don't want to go out of their way. Teach children the safe way to cross in the middle of the street. Pick a good spot to cross (very little traffic, a clear view, etc.). Walk up to the curb with the child and together say "STOP" as you stop. Tell the child to "LOOK, first to the left, then to the right and the left again while LISTENING for traffic." Gently turn the child's head to show him/her how far to LOOK in each direction. Make sure the child can tell the difference between parked cars and moving cars. Test the child by asking him/her to take you by the hand and help you cross the street (make sure the way is clear and be ready to hold the child back if it isn't). When the child feels ready to cross the street alone, follow closely behind and correct any mistakes. Crossing the street when playing. Children may forget about safety rules when they are playing. Make a point to practice safety lessons during play, so that children learn that safety is a part of play. Tell the child to stay on the sidewalk while you put a toy on the opposite side of the street. Return to the child and ask him/her to fetch the toy while showing you the "Stop, Look and Listen" rules learned thus far. Insist that he/she say out loud every step in the process. When the child has reached the toy on the other side of the street, join him/her there and repeat the exercise, if necessary. Do the exercise a final time, switching roles so that the child can grade you on how well you have followed the rules. Crossing the street with a crossing guard. It is a crossing guard's job to help school children cross the street, yet sometimes children don't know how to follow the guard's signals. Also, children who use a crossing guard may forget their safety rules when the guard is off duty. Teach children to respect the role of the crossing guard and to understand his/her signals. Remind children that the rules of crossing safely also apply when the guard is absent. Crossing at intersections with traffic light s. Children may be confused by traffic lights and not obey the signals. Teach children the exact meaning of each signal and remind them that the signals are not a replacement for safety habits like "Stop, Look and Listen." Stand with the child in a safe place at an intersection with traffic lights. Do not let go of the child's hand. Without leaving your spot, watch as the traffic lights go through an entire cycle. Explain first the signals for the cars: RED, YELLOW, and GREEN. Then explain the pedestrian signals: WALK, DON'T WALK. Reinforce that WALK also means DON'T RUN. If a signal appears when the child is part way across, he/she must keep on crossing and not run. Teach the child that at some traffic lights, they must push a button and wait for the WALK signal. Reinforce that STOP, LOOK and LISTEN always applies, even with a WALK signal. Point to a car turning into the crosswalk and show how it is a danger to people crossing. Explain that eye contact with the driver is the only way to be sure that the driver has seen you. Reminding the child that KID BRAKES always apply, walk back from the corner about 30 feet and approach again. Ask the child to lead you by the hand across the street at the next safe time (WALK signal). Correct any mistakes. Crossing at a corner with no traffic lights. A corner without lights can be scary for a child. There is no WALK signs to give the child "permission" to cross, and cars come from four directions. Also, drivers can be careless when not guided by traffic lights. Teach children to be extra alert in this situation, to follow the rules without exception and to pay special attention to drivers. Crossing between parked cars. Children should never be encouraged to cross between parked cars, which not only block their view, but also the view of oncoming drivers. However, they need to be prepared for those times when places to cross are limited. Teach children the safest way to cross. Explain why drivers cannot see small children between parked cars and why it is important for children to be on the alert. Back to top Crossing railway tracks. Many people ignore the crossing protection at railway crossings thinking that they can "beat" a speeding train, either on foot or in their cars. Many are killed. Some children also think it's an adventure to walk along railway tracks or to cross railway tracks in unmarked areas. Both habits are not only extremely dangerous; they are against the law. Remind children that playing games at railway crossings or around trains can be deadly. Trains travel at different speeds and in both directions on as many as 5 or 6 tracks. Teach children that the only safe way to cross is to use designated railway crossings, and obey all signals and/or signage. - Visit a public rail crossing in your area likely to be used by your child. - STOP, LOOK AND LISTEN for a train approaching from both directions. - When you see a train in the distance, ask the child to LISTEN carefully to the sound it makes. - Point out what happens when the crossing protection receives the signal that a train is coming - flashing lights, bell and/or gate. - Note; Crossing protection varies. - As the train passes, point out that the train travels fast and cannot stop quickly. - Once the train has passed, STOP, LOOK AND LISTEN for another train. Warn the child never to assume that there is just one train. - At crossings with lights and a bell, it is not safe to cross until flashing and ringing stops completely. - When the way is clear, walk across the tracks. Remind the child how to walk safely. Walking on and crossing rural roads. There can be no one guide to traffic safety in rural areas. Conditions change from one road to the next. Safety rules must be customized to each child and the routes he/she uses. Get to know the child's routes in detail. Talk about the special conditions you see and how to deal with them. Join with neighbourhood kids and their parents to come up with a safety plan that works for the roads in your area. Testing safety habits: walking alone or with friends. A child cannot be supervised 24 hours a day, every day. There will be many times when a child is walking alone or with friends. Be sure that children are practicing their safety rules. First, make up safety quizzes on your walks around the neighbourhood - ask the child to point out the safety places to cross, for example. Second, watch what the child does on his/her regular routes. - Walking alone - Plan a walk in your neighbourhood, which takes you and the child to many streets and corners. Running errands is a simple way to do this. - Ask the child to choose the route to each place you have to go and then let the child lead you. Correct mistakes and practice. - Along the way, have the child is comfortable taking the lead. - On another day, repeat the exercise while watching the child from a distance (no more than a half block) - Walking with friends - Plan a walk with a group of the child's friends. The route home from school is a good choice. (Children are most distracted after school. They want to run and have fun. The chance of traffic injury is greater at this time than before school.) - Ask the children to lead you. - Follow the "walking alone" activity above. Back to top - In areas without sidewalks, teach children to walk next to the road, in single file and facing traffic. If they are forced to walk on the road, they should stay as close to the side of the road as possible. - Watch the child at play to be sure he/she is suing KID BRAKES. - On walks around the neighbourhood, show the child how parked cars, curves in the road, hills and trees can block the view of oncoming traffic. Explain that these are dangerous areas to cross. - There may be no official plan of action if a crossing guard is absent or if the children are dismissed from school earlier than usual. - Talk to your child, other parents and the school about what to do if this happens. - Warn the child never to cross on a diagonal (j-walk). - Warn the child that it is NEVER SAFE TO STAND OR WALK BETWEEN CARS IN PARKING LOTS. - ALL WEATHER ALERT: Make sure you repeat Activity 1 & 2 in bad weather. If you see children following safety rules in the snow and rain - when there are more dangers to think about Safety professionals warn that children under the age of 7 should not cross any streets alone, and that children under the age of 9 should not cross busy streets alone. - Find ways to practice traffic safety during the child's daily activities on walks to the park, to the store, to school etc. Keep on practicing. Keep safety messages fresh. - Set a good example! Children will copy behavior, good or bad. - Tell babysitters, grandparents and neighbors the same messages you tell your children. - Remember that children judge distance, speed and sounds differently than adults. - Be patient and praise, praise, praise. - Safety Habits to be learned - STOP before stepping onto the road - LOOK all ways - LISTEN for vehicles which can't be seen - Check that cars have been stopped - Make eye contact with drivers - Obey traffic signs AN IMPORTANT NOTE ABOUT PARKING LOTS Parking lots are very dangerous places. A lot of cars are moving around in a small space and drivers are often distracted. Parked cars can suddenly stop. - Hold the child's hand at all times - If you are holding bags in both hands, ask the child (or children) to hold onto your clothing or hug your legs - When you get to your car, put the child in the car before you load the bags (rest the bags on the ground next to the car until you're ready). Learning traffic signs: - Point out traffic signs in the neighbourhood and explain what they mean - Quiz the child during walks and drives around town - Be sure to include a full variety of signs in your quizzes, even if it means going out of your way during walks or drives - Ask the child to make a "nest" with his/her hands. Place an imaginary egg in the child's "nest" and ask him/her to walk across the yard or driveway without "breaking the egg." Follow closely behind. Listening for traffic: - Stand on the sidewalk holding the child's hand. Ask the child to close his/her eyes and tell you when he/she hears a car coming. - Learning the difference between moving cars and parked cars: - Find a safe place off the road (benches at a bus stop, for example). Do not let go of the child's hand. - Looking down the street, say the rhyme, "I spy with my little eye something that is green." And it is a parked car." Ask the child to point it out. - Changer the rhyme: "I spy with my little eye something that is red. And it is a moving car." Ask the child to point it out. - Ask the child to "test" you with the rhyme. Continue with the game until he/she can tell the difference between parked cars and moving cars - especially slow-moving cars. Back to top The ideal crossing: flashing lights, bells, and a gate, which descends. Usually located at busy crossings in well populated areas. Similar to Type 1, except without the gate. Usually located in well-populated areas at crossings with moderate traffic. Consists of a reflectorized reflective "crossbuck" only, without lights, bells, or a gate. Located in rural areas and places where there is minimal rail traffic. ALWAYS STEP OVER THE RAILS Stepping on top of a rail is very dangerous - a child's foot can slip and become wedged in the groove. NEVER RIDE A BIKE over the tracks. A tire can get caught in the rails and cause the cyclist to fall. For more information on railway safety visit the CN website. CN has a wonderful site where kids can enter a wonderful world of fun and learn about railway safety at the same time. Click here to link to this website. No Sidewalks and No Curbs: Walking - Teach the child to walk beside the road, in single file and facing traffic. If they are forced to walk on the road, they should stay as close to the edge of the road as possible. In winter, decide how to deal with snow banks. Curves in the road, trees and snow banks can block a child's view of oncoming traffic. Point out the best places to cross and the areas never to cross. Make sure the child understands exactly what it is that is blocking his/her view so he/she can look for problems on other routes. Can travel faster on rural roads and so a child must wait for a larger break in traffic to cross safely (without running). To measure the time it takes to do so, cross the road together while counting "One Thousand, etc." method. Practice until the child understands how much time is needed. Rural roads often do not have enough street lamps and in the winter it gets dark early. Teach the child to be extra alert at these times and to always wear brightly colored clothes (they stand out against snow, too) or preferably, clothes with reflective stripes. Point out anything in your area that is a hazard to the child, including farm vehicles, construction equipment, hidden intersections and railway crossings. Always be on the lookout for changes to the area (road repair, for example) that should be discussed with the child Download a copy of Kidestrian Back to top It's just one of those things that we're working on. This section contains all the display-related settings. 3. **Tap on Daydream:** Look for 'Daydream' in this menu and select it. If you can't find it, it might be under 'Advanced'. 4. **Enable Daydream:** If Daydream is currently off, toggle the switch at the top of the page to turn it on. Title: How to Get Your Parents to Allow You to Paint Your Nails Any Color Introduction: Painting your nails can be a fun and creative way to express yourself. However, if you're underage, you might need to convince your parents first. This guide will provide you with steps to help you communicate effectively with your parents about this topic, understand their concerns, negotiate, and reach an agreement that satisfies both parties. Remember, patience and respect are crucial throughout this process. Step 1: Understand Their Perspective Before approaching your parents, take some time to think about why they may have reservations regarding nail polish. Some common reasons include: - Concerns about hygiene (e.g., chipped or dirty nail polish) - Worries about chemicals found in certain brands - Belief that nail polish is inappropriate for children - Fear of setting a precedent for other appearance modifications By acknowledging these potential concerns beforehand, you demonstrate maturity and empathy, which will make negotiations easier. Step 2: Research Appropriate Brands and Colors Find reputable brands known for safe ingredients, such as water-based or kid-friendly options. Choose colors that align with situations where your parents expect you to look neat and presentable, like school or family events. By doing so, you show responsibility and consideration for their opinions. Key Tip: Avoid overly flashy or dark colors initially; start with more subtle shades to ease them into the idea. Step 3: Prepare Your Argument Write down your thoughts about why painting your nails would benefit you. Consider discussing aspects such as self-expression, creativity, building confidence, or even bonding opportunities when applying nail polish together. Use specific examples from your life to support your argument. Be prepared to listen actively to any counterarguments they might raise and respond calmly and thoughtfully. Step 4: Request a Meeting Ask your parents for a dedicated conversation on this subject at a convenient time when everyone is relaxed and free from distractions. Explain that you want to discuss something important to you and ensure they feel heard during the discussion. Step 5: Present Your Case During the meeting, share your research, reasons, and chosen brand/colors. Listen attentively to their feedback and address any concerns raised. Offer compromises, like only wearing nail polish on weekends or agreeing to remove it promptly if asked. Demonstrating flexibility shows that you value their input and are willing to work collaboratively towards a solution. Step 6: Negotiate and Agree on Terms Work together to establish ground rules surrounding nail polish application. For example, you could agree on appropriate occasions to wear nail polish, acceptable color palettes, or regular maintenance schedules. Make sure both parties are satisfied with the agreed terms before moving forward. Guidelines: - Always maintain good hygiene by keeping your nails clean and trimmed. - Remove chipped polish immediately. - Stick to the agreed-upon schedule for applying and removing nail polish. - Respect their decisions and engage in open communication. Step 7: Follow Through Adhere strictly to the established agreements to prove your commitment and trustworthiness. Show appreciation for their understanding and support by consistently demonstrating responsible behavior. Over time, this consistency may encourage them to allow greater freedom in your choices. Conclusion: Gaining permission to paint your nails any color requires patience, preparation, and effective communication. By following these steps and maintaining a positive attitude, you increase the likelihood of reaching an amicable agreement with your parents while fostering mutual respect and understanding. In 2020, Roger Penrose, Reinhard Genzel and Andrea Ghez were awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics for their work on the theory of black holes. Imagine you are a traveling salesperson who needs to visit several cities before returning home. Your goal is to find the shortest possible route that takes you to every city once and brings you back home. This problem is called the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). Let's see how we can turn it into a simpler problem using some basic ideas! 1. **City Labeling:** First, assign a number to each city, starting from 0 up to the total number of cities, which we will call `n`. Now our job is to create a sequence or "path" that visits each city exactly once and returns to the starting point (city 0). 2. **Path Variables:** Introduce variables named `x_ij` for each pair of cities `(i, j)`, where `i` and `j` represent two different cities. The value of `x_ij` tells us whether the path goes directly from city `i` to city `j`: * When the path does go from city `i` to city `j`, then `x_ij = 1`. * Otherwise, when the path doesn't go from city `i` to city `j`, then `x_ij = 0`. 3. **Distance Function:** We need a function to calculate the total distance traveled. Using the distances between each pair of cities, denoted by `c_ij`, multiply these values by their corresponding `x_ij` variables. Summing over all pairs of cities gives us the total distance the salesperson would travel. Our objective is to minimize this sum while visiting all cities once and coming back to city 0. 4. **Constraints:** To ensure the correct sequence of visiting cities, add constraints like these: * Each city should have one incoming edge and one outgoing edge, except for city 0, which serves both as start and end points. Use constraint equations involving `x_ij` variables accordingly. 5. **Artificial Variables:** Add extra integer variables called "artificial" variables, `u_i`, to avoid subtracting constants within inequality constraints later on. These won't affect the solution but make things easier mathematically. 6. **Final Constraint:** Include another set of inequality constraints that allows forming loops without creating unwanted smaller cycles. This prevents the salesperson from getting stuck in certain parts of the graph unnecessarily. Now your task is transformed into finding optimal values for these variables - specifically, minimizing the total distance based on the given conditions. While solving this simplified version may not always yield the quickest real-world solution due to its complexity, understanding the concept helps build intuition towards more advanced optimization methods.
