" Title: How to Flirt with a Girl on the Phone Introduction: Flirting over the phone can be both exciting and nerve-wracking, especially if you're new to it or unsure about what to say. However, with some practice and these simple steps, you can turn an ordinary phone call into a flirty conversation that could potentially lead to something more serious. The following guide will help you navigate through this process while ensuring clarity and practicality. Step 1: Set the Right Mood Before making the call, ensure you are in a comfortable and quiet environment where you won't be disturbed. This sets the stage for a relaxed and engaging conversation. Dimming the lights or lighting candles might also add to the romantic atmosphere. Desired Outcome: Creating a conducive environment allows you to focus on your conversation and creates a sense of intimacy. Key Tip: Avoid noisy public places as they may distract both of you from having a meaningful discussion. Step 2: Start with Casual Conversation Begin by asking her how her day has been or talking about shared interests. This eases tension and establishes rapport. Desired Outcome: Starting casually makes her feel at ease and opens up opportunities for flirting later in the conversation. Step 3: Use Humor Incorporate light humor into your conversation. Making her laugh is a great way to break down barriers and create connection. Just make sure it's appropriate and respectful. Desired Outcome: Humor can diffuse any nervousness and shows her that you're fun to be around. Step 4: Compliment Her Genuine compliments are powerful tools when flirting. They show that you appreciate her and find her attractive. Focus on aspects such as her personality, intelligence, talents, or style rather than just physical appearance. Desired Outcome: Compliments boost her self-esteem and make her see you in a positive light. Step 5: Engage in Playful Banter Tease her gently about things like her favorite sports team losing or her taste in music. Keep it friendly and good-natured; never cross the line into insults or sarcasm. Desired Outcome: Playful banter can spark chemistry and inject excitement into your interaction. Step 6: Show Interest in Her Life Ask questions about her hobbies, dreams, and aspirations. Listen carefully to her responses and respond thoughtfully. Sharing about yourself too is important so she feels heard and understood. Desired Outcome: Demonstrating genuine interest indicates that you value her opinions and want to know more about her. Step 7: Drop Hints About Your Intentions Subtly hint at your intentions without being explicit. For example, mention plans for a future date or express hope for another call. Desired Outcome: Dropping hints gives her a clear signal of your interest without coming off too strong. Step 8: Read Her Responses Pay attention to her tone, words, and reactions during the conversation. If she seems uncomfortable or uninterested, respect her feelings and change tactic or end the call gracefully. Desired Outcome: Reading her cues helps maintain a pleasant conversation and avoid misunderstandings. Conclusion: Remember, every person is unique, so adapt these steps according to your understanding of her preferences and comfort level. Authenticity goes a long way when flirting, so be true to yourself and enjoy the process! That was like the beach this summer. Once upon a time there was a little girl. She loved to make things. Title: How to Draw the Letter M in Bubble Text Bubble text is a fun and creative way to write letters or words with rounded edges and distinct shapes, making them appear as if they are floating bubbles. This type of lettering can be used for invitations, greeting cards, posters, and more. Here's an in-depth guide on drawing the letter 'M' in bubble text step by step. **Step 1: Familiarize yourself with basic bubble text principles.** Before starting, understand that bubble text consists of oval shapes connected smoothly without any sharp corners or angles. The thickness (width) of lines should remain consistent throughout your design. Also, remember that curved lines need to flow seamlessly into straight ones and vice versa. **Step 2: Sketch two overlapping circles.** Start by sketching two overlapping circles horizontally using light pencil strokes. These will serve as guides for forming the upper and lower parts of the letter 'M'. Make sure the left circle is slightly higher than the right one, which sets up the necessary asymmetry for the letter shape. **Step 3: Outline the top half of the M.** Now, draw an elongated teardrop shape above the left circle, connecting its ends to the outer edge of the right circle. This creates the first part of the 'M'. Note that this line should have equal distance from both circles' rims. **Step 4: Form the middle section of the M.** Underneath the connection point between the two circles, create another curved line extending towards the right side but stopping short of touching the bottom rim of the right circle. This forms the bridge of the 'M'. **Step 5: Complete the bottom half of the M.** Similar to Step 3, form an elongated teardrop shape below the previously drawn curve and connect its ends to the outer edge of the right circle. This completes the second part of the 'M'. **Step 6: Thicken the outlines.** Go over all your sketched lines carefully, adding some width to make them bolder and more prominent. Keep the thickness uniform across all sections. Erase any unnecessary guidelines at this stage. **Step 7: Smooth out curves and edges.** Ensure all curves are smooth and transitions between straight and curved lines are fluid. Remove any irregularities or rough patches with gentle erasures while maintaining the overall structure of the 'M'. **Step 8: Add shading (optional).** If you want to give your bubble text some dimension, add shadows or highlights using light pencil strokes. Typically, shadows fall on the sides opposite to the light source, creating a sense of depth. By following these steps meticulously, you can successfully draw the letter 'M' in bubble text. Remember to maintain consistency in line thickness, ensure smooth connections between lines, and consider adding shadow effects for enhanced visual appeal. With practice, you'll master this playful yet stylish font! A woman's orgasm begins in her clitoris, which is the counterpart of the man's penis. It is preceded by an erection of the clitoris and a moistening of the opening of the vagina. When a woman goes through a full orgasm, her uterus, vagina, anus and pelvic muscles undergo a series of rhythmic contractions. Most women find these contractions very pleasurable. Rubbing or applying other consistent pressure against the clitoris is usually needed to help the woman have an orgasm. Why do they show just the XXX? And there's a sea lion [seal honks]. D' you want to listen to my heartbeat? The green cross code Children need to learn how to cross the road safely. In 2010, around 19 child pedestrians (aged 0-11) were killed or seriously injured every week in Britain. The Green Cross Code is taught in schools but needs to be reinforced from home - and not just by memorising the words, but by parents practicing and explaining the code to their children. Do you know it? Do you follow it? The code is for everyone. The Green Cross Code 1. First find the safest place to cross - If possible, cross the road at: subways, footbridges, islands, zebra, puffin, pelican or toucan crossings, or where there is a crossing point controlled by a police officer, a school crossing patrol or a traffic warden. - Otherwise, choose a place where you can see clearly in all directions, and where drivers can see you. - Try to avoid crossing between parked cars and on sharp bends or close to the top of a hill. Move to a space where drivers and riders can see you clearly. - There should be space to reach the pavement on the other side. 2. Stop just before you get to the kerb - Do not get too close to the traffic. If there is no pavement, keep back from the edge of the road but make sure you can still see approaching traffic. - Give yourself lots of time to have a good look all around. 3. Look all around for traffic and listen - Look all around for traffic and listen. - Look in every direction. - Listen carefully because you can sometimes hear traffic before you can see it. 4. If traffic is coming, let it pass - Look all around again and listen. - Do not cross until there is a safe gap in the traffic and you are certain that there is plenty of time. - Remember, even if traffic is a long way off, it may be approaching very quickly. 5. When it is safe, go straight across the road - do not run - Keep looking and listening for traffic while you cross, in case there is any traffic you did not see, or in case other traffic appears suddenly. - Look out for cyclists and motorcyclists traveling between lanes of traffic. - Do not cross diagonally. How you can help your child and other children - Set a good example. Use the Green Cross Code yourself. - Show your child how to use the Code to cross the road when you're out and about. - Let your child show you that they know how to cross the road safely - start practising on quiet roads first. - Point out dangerous places to cross on local roads. Point out safer places as well. Some places may be safer at some times of the day than at others. - Use pedestrian crossings even if it involves a small detour. - Talk about the importance of not using a mobile phone or texting while crossing the road. - Remind your child that they cannot hear traffic if listening to music through earphones or see it properly if wearing a large hood. But let's get one thing clear: it's still important for children to be outside. Walking is good for children's health and fitness and we support parents who encourage their children to walk as much as possible. Taking your child in the car for short journeys puts more traffic on the road and adds to the problem. Children can be safer on the streets if we show them how. Crossing between parked cars Try not to cross between parked vehicles, but if there is nowhere else to cross: - Choose a place where there is a space between two cars and make sure that it is easy to get to the pavement on the other side of the road. - Make sure neither car is about to move off - look for drivers in the cars, lights and listen for engines. - Don't cross near large vehicles. You could be standing in a blind spot, where the driver cannot see you. - Walk to the outside edge of the cars and stop. Here you can be seen by drivers and you can look all around for traffic. - Use the Green Cross Code. When the road is clear, cross, still looking and listening as you go. Frog Pond Life Cycle Model Materials: non-waxed paper plates, crayons (no shades of blue), small sponges, watered down blue tempera paint, small containers to hold the paint, small "flippy frogs" - 1 per child Pass out the crayons and the plates. Explain that frogs begin their life in the water and that baby frogs come from eggs. The eggs are laid in a group. Animals that begin their life in the water and then live on land are called amphibians. Baby frogs are called tadpoles. They don't look like their parents. Ask the children to take a dark crayon and draw frog eggs in Then have them draw three tadpoles. Give one frog hind legs. Then give one frog hind and front legs. Talk about the things that a tadpole would need to survive in its habitat. Tadpoles eat plants. They are vegetarians. The children will need to draw some plants. As a tadpole gets older, it will begin to prey on insects, worms, and even small fish. Ask the children to use their crayons to make a log for the adult frog to sit on to warm itself in the sun. Amphibians are ectotherms. Their body temperature is the same as the outside air. Then have them draw some bugs in the water. Adult frogs catch insects with their sticky Help the children dip the ends of the small sponges into the paint and paint over their "pond." (The sponge needs to be only slightly damp with the paint.) These will dry quickly. Give each child a frog and let them see if they can get it into their "pond." Clear the Spotlight Search History in iOS The Spotlight search feature in iOS is a powerful tool that allows you to quickly find contacts, apps, documents, and more by swiping down on your home screen or using the physical search button on your device. Over time, as you use this feature, your search history will accumulate, which can be both helpful and intrusive. If you want to clear your Spotlight search history to maintain your privacy or declutter the list, here's an easy-to-follow, step-by-step guide: 1. **Unlock your iPhone or iPad.** To begin clearing your Spotlight search history, first ensure your device is unlocked and at its home screen. This step guarantees you have access to all available options and settings necessary to complete the process. 2. **Swipe down on the home screen.** Place your finger near the center top edge of the display and gently pull downwards until you see the Spotlight search bar appear. By doing so, you open the Spotlight search interface, where you can view and manage your search history. 3. **Tap the 'X' next to individual items (optional).** You may notice grayed out entries representing past searches below the Spotlight search bar. Next to these entries are small 'x' icons. Tapping any 'x' icon removes only that specific item from your Spotlight search history without affecting other entries. Use this option if you wish to remove selective items instead of erasing everything. 4. **Reset all Spotlight suggestions.** If you prefer to delete every entry within your Spotlight search history, proceed with resetting all Spotlight suggestions. Access Settings by tapping the gear icon found either on your home screen or via Control Center. 5. **Navigate to Siri & Search section in Settings.** Scroll through the menu until locating 'Siri & Search.' Selecting this option provides control over various aspects related to Siri functionality, including managing your Spotlight search preferences and data. 6. **Select 'Search History.'** Within the Siri & Search menu, scroll down until finding the subheading titled 'SEARCH HISTORY.' Beneath it lies a single toggle switch labeled 'Show Siri & Suggestions in App Library & Spotlight,' followed by 'Search History' directly beneath. Choose 'Search History' to continue. 7. **Toggle off 'Show Siri & Suggestions in App Library & Spotlight.'** Before deleting your entire Spotlight search history, turn off the main toggle switch above the list of previous searches. Disabling this setting prevents new data collection while working to protect user privacy. Once disabled, tap 'Clear History' located just above the toggle switch. A confirmation message appears; choose 'Clear History' once again to finalize removal. 8. **Verify deleted history.** After completing steps six and seven, navigate back to the Spotlight search function by swiping downward on your home screen. Confirm whether your Spotlight search history has been successfully cleared. The absence of previously displayed entries indicates successful completion of this procedure. Key Tips and Guidelines: * Be aware that disabling 'Show Siri & Suggestions in App Library & Spotlight' also affects other features beyond simply removing your Spotlight search history. For example, app recommendations based on usage patterns might no longer appear when performing searches. * Clearing your Spotlight search history does not affect Safari web browsing history, suggesting websites, or frequently visited sites. These separate entities require distinct methods to manage their respective histories. * Keep in mind that regularly maintaining digital hygiene practices like periodically clearing your Spotlight search history contributes positively toward preserving personal information security and overall system performance optimization. Now just hurry up and eat Chi. Um and she's got a Goodnight Moon book. Title: Solving the Cake Puzzle: A Grade School Approach Have you ever heard the story of someone being trapped inside a cake? It sounds funny, doesn't it? But let's imagine something similar for a moment. Imagine yourself in the center of a big cube of cake, and your goal is to eat your way out without going back to any part you've already eaten. You can only move in six directions - up, down, left, right, forward, and backward. Can you do it? Let's explore this fun problem together! First, think about when you walk around your neighborhood. To get home, sometimes you don't just go straight; instead, you take turns walking along streets and avenues. This idea helps us think about our cake challenge differently. Instead of only moving along one direction at once, we can combine them - taking two steps at the same time, like stepping to the left and back simultaneously. We call these combined moves "diagonal steps." Now, let's try solving the cake puzzle using diagonal steps. When standing in the middle of the cake, here's a step-by-step guide on eating your way out while avoiding repeats: 1. Begin by eating a bit of the cake directly in front of you (let's call this positive Z). Now, to avoid doubling back, also eat a little bit of the cake to your left (+Z and +Y) and right (+Z and -Y). You now moved three spaces without crossing your path! 2. Continue making diagonal combinations along new paths until you reach the edge of the cake block. Just remember to include all six possible directions during each turn so you cover everything evenly. By combining several directions at once, you create a zigzag pattern through the cake. Following this plan ensures every bite is unique, allowing you to finish the whole cake without getting stuck or retracing your steps. This exciting adventure in edible exploration teaches us about creative thinking and problem-solving-two essential skills useful both inside classrooms and beyond. And who knows? Maybe next time you face a tricky challenge, you too could find inspiration from a delicious source like a big, yummy cake! Yes how's the fast car go? Rolling Ball Size Name: Lee S. Does a small ball roll faster than a larger ball? If so why or why not? I think that a small ball rolls faster because it turns over quicker? Is that right? The answer depends upon what you mean by "faster". If you mean that a small ball and a large ball are moving at the same number of revolutions/second, then the larger ball will move a greater distance than the small ball in the same length of time, so you would say the large ball moved faster -- greater distance in the same time. If you mean that a small ball and a large ball move the same distance in the same time, the small ball will have to make more revolutions in that time in order to travel the same distance as the large ball so it travels at more revolutions/second -- same distance in the same time, more revolutions per Of course the time did not have to be seconds, it could have been minutes or This question is incomplete. There is no definite speed at which a ball rolls. How hard and long the ball is pushed, as well as the mass of the ball, will be important. Let us assume all things are equal (starting speed, weight, both balls solid, path the ball follows) except for size. One ball is larger than the other. On Earth, it is true that the large ball slows more than the small ball. This is because the larger ball runs into more air molecules. For a ball to move, it has to push the air out of the way. This results in air resistance, the same force that slows a man with a parachute. The larger ball feels more air resistance. On the moon, where there is no air, both balls would roll the same. I am not sure what the strating conditions of your balls are. However, one of the most famous experiments ever performed was by Galileo, some 400 years ago. He wanted to know whether heavy objects fell faster than light ones. The famous story is that he went to the top of the leaning tower of Pisa and dropped two objects, one heavy and one light. He saw that they fell at the same rate! It has been shown that this was just a story because Galileo did not have any way to measure the rate at which the objects fell (he did not have a camera or other high speed recording device). What he did do is make a ramp out of wood, and roll balls of various weights down this ramp. The ramp allowed him extra time to measure how long it took for the balls to reach the bottom. It took the same length of time, no matter how heavy the balls were. This was the basis for a well-known law of acceleration due to gravity. If you mean which ball rolls faster along a flat surface, that is a bit different. If the two balls turn over at the same rate, then the bigger ball will move farther in the same length of time. Let us say that the radius of the small ball is r, and the radius of the large ball is R. The circumference of a ball is (2 times Pi times radius). This means that each time a ball turns over, the small ball will move (2 times Pi times r), while the big ball will move (2 times Pi times R). Since R is bigger than r, the big ball will go farther. Drama Lessons for Foundation Stage and Key Stage 1 based on The Gruffalo by Julia Donaldson (Macmillan 1999). Learning objectives (linked to objectives from the Primary Framework for Literacy and Mathematics): Below are some simple ways to use drama activities to enhance a storytelling session. Meet a Creature Begin by reading the first part of The Gruffalo to the children up until the end of the first meeting with the fox. Show them the pictures and discuss the story with them as usual. Now you are going to introduce the techniques of Teacher in Role and Hot-seating. No acting skills are required, you simply talk to the children as if you were a character. This is only a small step from reading the story aloud. Show the children the double page spread before the title page and ask them what creatures they can see there (a butterfly, a beetle and a woodpecker.) Ask them what questions they would put to any of these characters if they met them, about what has happened in the story. Explain that you are going to pretend to be one of these creatures and that you are going to show when you are pretending to be the character by putting on a special hat (or other piece of token costume). You can choose which of the three creatures to be. Step away from your chair and put on the hat (a baseball cap might suit the woodpecker). Sit down again and begin by saying 'hello' as the character. You do not need to act, or to move in a special way or even use a particular voice. Simply give the point of view of that character and his or her feelings. You can give the children clues about who you are by telling them where you live and what you have seen. Encourage them to find out who you are and to pose questions about what you have seen. Ask pupils questions too. In this way you can encourage the children to recap on the story so far by questioning them about why the fox ran away. It's up to you how much detail you ask for. You could finish by quoting the mouse's words: Silly old Fox! Doesn't he know, There's no such thing as a gruffalo? Then simply say goodbye and remove the hat to signify that you are stepping out of role. You can continue in this way through the story, taking different roles, including the fox, the owl, the snake, the mouse and the Gruffalo. Explore facial expressions of characters at various points in the story. Invite the children to stand in a circle. Explain that they are going to use their faces to show how characters felt at different times in the story. Choose a character and a moment, for example the fox when he first met the mouse. Count down Three-Two-One and clap your hands or shake a tambourine as a signal for them to make the face. You can pick out some of the expressions to show the others. How would they describe the feelings being shown on other pupils' faces? Then choose a different moment/animal, for example the fox when he sees the Gruffalo. These drama activities are taken from Learning Through Drama in the Primary Years by David Farmer. The complete lesson plan includes such activities as Sounds of the Forest, Exploring the Woods, Story Moments, Meet the Mouse, Gruffalo's Footsteps, Meet the Gruffalo and Advice Line. Title: Understanding the Perimeter of a Regular Polygon Hello young mathematicians! Today, we're going to learn about calculating the perimeter of a regular polygon. A polygon is just a fancy name for a shape with straight lines, like triangles, squares, or pentagons. When we talk about a "regular" polygon, we mean that all its sides and angles are equal. Let's imagine you have a circular cookie cutter, and you want to make shapes with n number of sides - triangles (3 sides), squares (4 sides), pentagons (5 sides), and so on. Place the cookie cutter on the dough, and there you have your regular polygon inscribed in a circle! Now, let's think about one side of this regular polygon. To find the length of one side, we need to draw two perpendicular lines from the center of the circle to the two ends of the side. These perpendicular lines will bisect (cut in half) the side, forming two smaller right triangles inside our larger polygon. Using these right triangles, we can apply some cool geometry concepts called the RHS Criterion of Congruency and CPCT (Corresponding Parts of Congruent Triangles). By doing so, we discover that the central angle formed by one side of the polygon measures (n-2)*90 / n. With this information, we can now determine the ratio between the length of the polygon's side (BL) and the radius of the circle (r): BL / r = cos((n-2)*90 / n) From here, finding the length of one side (BL) and multiplying it by 2 gives us the length of the entire side (BC): BC = 2 * BL = 2 * r * cos((n-2)*90 / n) Lastly, since there are n sides in our regular polygon, to get the total perimeter, we simply multiply the length of one side by n: Perimeter = n * BC = 2 * n * r * cos((n-2)*90 / n) And that's it! You've learned how to calculate the perimeter of any regular polygon using its number of sides, the radius of the surrounding circle, and a bit of fun geometry! Keep practicing and exploring shapes; who knows what other exciting ideas await in the world of mathematics? Sam and Ben were brothers who liked to play in the park. One day, they saw a big slide with a ladder and a tunnel. Sam wanted to go first, but Ben said he was too weak to climb the ladder. "I'm not weak, you're weak!" Sam said. He ran to the ladder and started to climb. Ben followed him, but he was slower. When Sam reached the top of the slide, he saw something shiny in the tunnel. He crawled inside and found a metal spike sticking out of the wall. He touched it and felt a sharp pain in his hand. He cried out and tried to pull his hand away, but the spike was stuck. "Help! Help!" Sam shouted. Ben heard him and climbed into the tunnel. He saw Sam's hand bleeding and the spike holding it. He was scared, but he wanted to help his brother. "Sam, don't move!" Ben said. He grabbed Sam's hand and gave it a hard shake. The spike came loose and Sam's hand was free. Sam hugged Ben and thanked him. "Let's go home, Sam," Ben said. "We need to put a bandage on your hand." "OK, Ben," Sam said. "You're not weak, you're brave." They slid down the slide and ran to their mom, who was waiting for them. She saw their faces and knew something had happened. It was given to the United States by the people of France in 1886, to represent the friendship between the two countries established during the American Revolution. It represents a woman wearing a stola, a crown and sandals, trampling a broken chain, and with a torch in her raised right hand and a "tabula ansata", or tablet where the date of the Declaration of Independence "" (1776) is written, in her left hand. Scientifically, temperature is a physical quantity which describes how quickly molecules are moving inside a material. In solids and liquids the molecules are vibrating around a fixed point in the substance, but in gases they are in free flight and bouncing off each other as they travel. In a gas the temperature, pressure and volume of the gas are closely related by a law of physics. 550,000 are Pentecostals and Evangelicals (0.8%). 235,685 Jehovah's Witnesses (0.4%), 30,000 Waldensians, 25,000 Seventh-day Adventists, 22,000 Mormons, 20,000 Baptists, 7,000 Lutherans, 4,000 Methodists. The term organic originates from Jons Jacob Berzelius, a 19th century Swedish scientist, who used the term to refer to substances present in living things. During Berzelius' time, the vital force theory was popular. This theory stated that a "life force" was needed to produce the organic compounds found only in living things. The vital force theory began losing support after an 1828 experiment conducted by Friedrich W hler. His work showed that urea, an organic compound, could be created from ammonium cyanate, an inorganic compound. Nobody's gonna 'cause we don't have time. Either she's trying to take his stuff. A smiley (also called a "smiley face" and an "emoticon") is a picture of a smiling face that is used to show emotion. The first smileys to be widely used were made as yellow buttons, but now the most common smileys are made using computer keyboard symbols. Smileys are used by many people in emails and other types of computer messages. A "smiley" is also sometimes spelt wrongly as "smily" or "smilie". A liquid with ions is called an electrolyte. A gas with lots of ions is called a plasma. When ions move, it is called electricity. For example, in a wire, the metal ions do not move, but the electrons move as electricity. A positive ion and a negative ion will move together. Two ions of the same charge will move apart. When ions move they also make "magnetic fields". Title: How to Be Polite to Grandparents Introduction: Grandparents hold a special place in our hearts and families. They are repositories of family history, values, and traditions. Being polite to them is not only a mark of respect but also an opportunity to strengthen your bond with them. This tutorial will guide you through various steps to be polite to your grandparents. By following these steps, you can create meaningful relationships with them while showing gratitude and appreciation. Step 1: Address Them Appropriately Key Tip: Use proper titles such as 'Grandma' or 'Grampa', or their preferred names when addressing your grandparents. This shows that you acknowledge and respect their role in your life. If they have specific cultural or personal preferences regarding their title, make sure to honor those wishes. Guideline: Pay attention to how others address your grandparents and ask them about their preference if necessary. Using appropriate titles fosters a sense of closeness and connection. Step 2: Show Interest in Their Stories Key Tip: Listen attentively when your grandparents share stories from their past or talk about their experiences. Engage with them by asking questions and expressing curiosity. Guideline: Demonstrating interest in their lives validates their feelings and makes them feel valued. It allows you to learn more about your family heritage and understand where you come from. Step 3: Offer Assistance Key Tip: Proactively offer help around the house or run errands for your grandparents. Small acts of kindness go a long way in building strong relationships. Guideline: Consider your capabilities before offering assistance. Always ensure you don't overpromise and underdeliver. Helping out regularly creates opportunities for quality time together and strengthens bonds. Step 4: Communicate Regularly Key Tip: Maintain regular contact via phone calls, video chats, letters, or visits. Consistent communication keeps the relationship alive even when physical distance separates you. Guideline: Establish a routine for communicating based on both parties' comfort levels and schedules. Remember to initiate conversations rather than always waiting for them to reach out to you. Step 5: Respect Boundaries Key Tip: Understand and respect your grandparents' boundaries, whether related to privacy, beliefs, or lifestyle choices. Acknowledging these limits reinforces mutual respect and understanding. Guideline: When unsure about certain topics or situations, simply ask what they are comfortable discussing or doing. Over time, this awareness becomes second nature and contributes significantly to maintaining harmony. Step 6: Send Thoughtful Gifts Key Tip: Select thoughtful gifts that cater to their interests, hobbies, or needs. Personalized presents show effort and consideration. Guideline: Keep track of any hints dropped during conversations about things they might like or need. Plan ahead for birthdays, holidays, or anniversaries so your gift arrives on time. Step 7: Spend Quality Time Together Key Tip: Arrange activities that all generations can enjoy, such as board games, cooking sessions, or outdoor excursions. Shared experiences foster deeper connections and memories. Guideline: Consult your grandparents about their preferences and abilities before planning activities. Make adjustments accordingly to ensure everyone feels included and enjoys themselves. Conclusion: By consistently implementing these steps, you demonstrate politeness towards your grandparents, thereby nurturing stronger familial ties. These actions contribute positively to creating lasting memories and learning valuable lessons from previous generations. Title: How to Apply 1960s Style Eye Makeup ============================================= The 1960s was an iconic decade for fashion and beauty, with eye makeup being one of its most defining features. This tutorial will guide you through achieving that classic 1960s eye look, which is characterized by bold eyeliners, thick mascara, and pastel eyeshadows. ### Step 1: Preparation Before starting your makeup application, ensure your face is clean and moisturized. Applying eye shadow or liner on dry skin can lead to creasing and uneven color payoff. Use a lightweight moisturizer suitable for your skin type. Allow it some time to absorb into your skin before proceeding. ### Step 2: Primer Application Applying primer onto your lid creates a smooth base for the eyeshadow, helping it stay put longer without fading or creasing throughout the day. It also intensifies the pigment of the eyeshadow, giving you more vibrant colors. Dot a small amount of primer over your entire lid using your finger or a flat shader brush, then blend outwards towards your brow bone. ### Step 3: Eyeshadow Base Choose a matte nude or beige eyeshadow as your base color. This neutral shade evens out any discoloration on your lid while providing the perfect backdrop for brighter shadows. Sweep this all over your lid up to the brow bone with a fluffy blending brush. Remember to tap off excess product from the brush to avoid fallout. ### Step 4: Defining the Crease To add definition to the eyes, select a medium brown eyeshadow. Using a crease brush, gently carve out the socket line - where the eyeball meets the skull - focusing mainly on the outer corner of your eye. Blend well to create a soft gradient effect; harsh lines are unflattering and ageing. ### Step 5: Adding Depth Next, pick a darker brown or grey eyeshadow to deepen the outer V (the area at the outer edge) of your eye. With a pencil brush, concentrate the color here but remember to blend thoroughly into the transition created earlier. The aim is to give depth and dimension to the eyes. ### Step 6: Highlighting the Brow Bone Using a highlighting shade like champagne or vanilla, accentuate your brow bone to make your eyes appear bigger and more open. Apply this just below the arch of your eyebrows using a smaller concealer brush, extending slightly beyond both ends of your brows. ### Step 7: Liquid Liner Wing One of the standout characteristics of 1960s eye makeup is the winged liquid eyeliner. Start by drawing a thin line close to your upper lashes, beginning at the inner corner and moving outward. Once you reach the outer third of your eye, start sloping upward creating a diagonal line connecting to the end of your brow. Fill in the space between these two lines to complete the wing. For beginners, try sticking tape or business cards along the angle you want your wing to be at, then draw along the straight edge for precision. ### Step 8: Tightlining [long silence, dings, splash, sniff; tape OFF/ON at 2119; still in bath, toy dinging] No I'm not puttin' it up very high. Title: Benefits of Copy Typing Services: A Comprehensive Guide Introduction: Copy typing services involve converting handwritten or typed documents into editable digital text. This process is essential for various industries such as legal, medical, academic, and business sectors. The benefits of copy typing services are numerous, including improved efficiency, accuracy, accessibility, and cost savings. Here's an in-depth guide on the advantages of using copy typing services. Step 1: Improved Efficiency Handwritten documents can be time-consuming to read and interpret, leading to decreased productivity. By converting these documents into digital format through copy typing services, you can quickly search, edit, and share them with others, thereby increasing your overall efficiency. Key Tip: When selecting a copy typing service provider, ensure they have fast turnaround times without compromising quality. Step 2: Enhanced Accuracy Manual data entry often results in errors due to illegible handwriting or human fatigue. However, professional copy typists possess excellent keyboard skills and attention to detail, ensuring high levels of accuracy. Guideline: Always check if the service guarantees minimum error rates and provides proofreading as part of their package. Step 3: Increased Accessibility Digital texts created by copy typing services allow people with visual impairments to utilize assistive technologies like screen readers. Furthermore, digital files can be accessed from any device worldwide, promoting collaboration and convenience. Remember, always confirm whether the service supports different file formats suitable for diverse needs. Step 4: Cost Savings Investing in internal resources (software, training) for document conversion may prove expensive compared to outsourcing this task. Hiring a copy typing agency could save money in the long run while providing consistent quality. It's advisable to request quotes from several providers to compare prices before making a decision. Step 5: Preservation of Original Documents Original documents, especially historical ones, need preservation. Exposing them frequently to handling increases wear and tear. With copy typing services, you maintain the original condition of these vital records while still being able to utilize their content digitally. Be sure to discuss handling procedures with your chosen service provider. Step 6: Confidentiality & Security Many businesses deal with sensitive information that requires confidential handling during the transcription process. Reputable copy typing agencies will have strict data protection policies in place, securing client information at all stages of the process. Don't hesitate to ask about their security measures upfront. Step 7: Focus on Core Tasks By delegating document conversion tasks to professionals, you free up time to focus on core business activities. Delegation enhances specialization, which generally leads to better outcomes. Therefore, consider what added value you could bring to your organization by offloading administrative work onto experts. Conclusion: The benefits of copy typing services extend beyond mere transcribing; they contribute significantly towards improving operational efficiency, accuracy, accessibility, and cost savings. As technology advances, so too do the capabilities of these services, offering increasingly sophisticated solutions tailored to unique industry requirements. Whether you represent a small startup or large corporation, incorporating copy typing services into your operations might just provide the edge needed to stay ahead in today's competitive marketplace. Here is a detailed tutorial on how to build a pet rock playground: **Materials Needed:** * A selection of rocks of various sizes and shapes * A container or enclosure to house the playground (such as a large plastic storage bin or a custom-built wooden box) * Sand or gravel for the base of the playground * Small toys and accessories for the rock, such as tunnels, bridges, and ramps * Decorative elements, such as plants, stones, and figurines * Tools, such as a trowel, scoop, and glue gun **Step 1: Choose Your Rocks** The first step in building a pet rock playground is to select a variety of rocks to serve as the main features of the playground. Look for rocks that are smooth and free of sharp edges, as these will be safer and more comfortable for your pet rock to interact with. Consider choosing rocks in different shapes and sizes to add interest and diversity to the playground. **Step 2: Prepare the Base** Next, prepare the base of the playground by spreading a layer of sand or gravel evenly across the bottom of the container or enclosure. This will provide a stable and naturalistic surface for the rocks to rest on, and will also help to absorb any moisture that may accumulate over time. **Step 3: Arrange the Rocks** Now it's time to start arranging the rocks in the playground! Begin by placing the largest rocks first, using them to create interesting formations and structures. Then fill in around the larger rocks with smaller ones, creating a varied and dynamic landscape for your pet rock to explore. Be sure to leave plenty of open space for your pet rock to move around freely. **Step 4: Add Toys and Accessories** To make the playground even more engaging for your pet rock, consider adding small toys and accessories, such as tunnels, bridges, and ramps. These can provide additional opportunities for your pet rock to climb, hide, and play, and can help keep them entertained and active. Simply place the toys among the rocks, securing them in place if necessary with a little bit of glue from a hot glue gun. **Step 5: Decorate the Playground** Finally, decorate the playground with some finishing touches, such as plants, stones, and figurines. These can help to enhance the overall appearance of the playground, making it more visually appealing and inviting for both you and your pet rock. Use your imagination and have fun with this step - there's no limit to what you can create! **Key Tips and Guidelines:** * Always choose smooth and safe rocks for your pet rock's playground * Make sure to leave enough open space for your pet rock to move around comfortably * Secure any toys or accessories in place with a strong adhesive, such as hot glue * Regularly check the playground for signs of wear and tear, and repair or replace any damaged components as needed * Have fun and get creative with the design and decoration of your pet rock's playground! Follow @universetoday on Twitter Next in our tour through the planets: Mars. Also known as the Red Planet, Mars is the 4th planet from the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of 228 million km from the Sun. It's further from the Sun, and takes 668 days to complete an orbit around the Sun. Mars is one of the planets visible with the unaided eye, and so it has been known for thousands of years. Because of its red color, ancient people associated Mars with their gods of war. Mars is named after the Roman god of war, the same as the Greek god Ares. In many ways Mars is similar to Earth. Its day length is only 24.6 hours and its axial tilt is 25.2 , almost the same as the Earth's. With the axial tilt, Mars goes through seasons like the Earth; although they last longer because of the longer Martian year. But then Mars is different in many ways too. It has a thin atmosphere; only 1% the thickness of the Earth's atmosphere at sea level. And what little atmosphere Mars has is almost entirely carbon dioxide; it would be poisonous to breathe the air on Mars. And without a thick atmosphere, Mars gets bitterly cold at night. Temperatures in the day might rise as high as 10 C, but then dip down to almost -90 C at night, even near the equator in the summer. The surface of Mars is red because its rocks are covered in iron-oxide - it's rust. There are vast plains of drifting sand, stretches of broken volcanic rock, and craters large and small. Some of the most extreme geological features in the Solar System are on Mars. The tallest mountain, Olympus Mons is on Mars, towering 27 km above the surrounding landscape. The largest chasm in the Solar System is also on Mars; the Valles Marineris, stretching 4,000 km long. You can see Mars with the unaided eye when it's high and bright in the sky. Mars makes its closest approach to Earth every 26 months, and when it's close, it looks like a bright red star. In a small telescope, you can make out that Mars is a dull red disk, and if you have a moderately powerful telescope, you can even see the planet's polar ice caps. Of all the planets, Mars is the most studied. There have been dozens of missions sent to the planet, and many of them have been successful. There have been 6 missions that have landed on the planet and studied it close up. And there have been more that have gone into orbit around Mars, studying it from above and mapping its surface in incredible detail. Mars has two moons, Phobos and Deimos. They're just a few kilometers across, and look like captured asteroids. Mars has no rings. We've also recorded several episodes of Astronomy Cast about Mars. Listen here, Episode 52: Mars. Title: How to Build Skateboarding Ramps Building skateboard ramps can be an exciting and rewarding project. It allows you to create your own skating environment, tailored to your skill level and preferences. This comprehensive guide will walk you through the process, providing detailed steps and helpful tips along the way. **Things You'll Need:** * Plywood (3/4 inch thick) * 2x4s * Circular saw or hand saw * Drill * Screws (2-1/2 inches long) * Tape measure * Carpenter's square * Sandpaper * Optional: Paint or varnish **Step 1: Design Your Ramp** Before you start building, decide what kind of ramp you want. A quarter pipe is the simplest design, but you could also build a launch ramp, funbox, spine ramp, or even a halfpipe. Each type has its own challenges and requires different materials and dimensions. For this tutorial, we'll focus on a basic quarter pipe. **Key Tip**: Always consider safety when designing your ramp. Make sure it's stable, doesn't wobble, and has a smooth surface for riding. **Step 2: Gather Materials** Once you've designed your ramp, calculate the amount of plywood and 2x4s you'll need. The exact quantities depend on the size of your ramp. As a guideline, a small quarter pipe might require two sheets of plywood and several 2x4s. **Step 3: Cut the Pieces** Using a circular saw or hand saw, cut the plywood into pieces according to your design. You'll typically need a large piece for the slope (the "ramp" part), and a smaller one for the base. Make sure the edges are straight and clean. **Key Guideline**: When cutting the slope, remember that a steeper angle provides more air time, while a shallower angle makes it easier to ride up. A common starting point is a 15-degree angle. **Step 4: Create the Frame** Construct the frame using 2x4s. This will provide support and stability for your ramp. Attach the vertical supports to the base first, then add horizontal braces every few feet for added strength. **Key Tip**: Pre-drill holes before screwing pieces together to prevent splitting. Also, ensure all connections are tight and secure. **Step 5: Attach the Plywood** Place the larger plywood piece onto the frame, aligning the edges. Secure it with screws every six inches along the edges and every foot in the interior. Repeat this process for the base. **Step 6: Smooth Out Edges** Use sandpaper to smooth out any rough edges or splinters. Pay particular attention to the top edge of the ramp where you'll be landing. **Optional Step 7: Decorate** Add some personal touch by painting or varnishing your ramp. This isn't necessary, but it can make your ramp look more appealing and professional. **Final Check**: Before using your new ramp, double-check its stability and structural integrity. Give it a gentle push from different angles to see if it moves or wobbles. If everything seems solid, you're ready to roll! Remember, practice makes perfect - both in skateboarding and ramp construction. Don't be discouraged if your first attempt isn't perfect; keep learning, improving, and most importantly, have fun! what's he post say after that? Have you ever heard of trigonometry before? It's a branch of mathematics that deals with the relationships between the angles and sides of triangles. One really cool thing about trigonometry is that we can use something called trigonometric functions to find the ratios of the sides of a right triangle based on the size of one of its angles. There are three main trigonometric functions: sine (sin), cosine (cos), and tangent (tan). Each of these functions gives us a different ratio depending on which side of the triangle we're looking at. The sine of an angle is equal to the length of the opposite side divided by the length of the hypotenuse, the cosine is the length of the adjacent side divided by the length of the hypotenuse, and the tangent is the length of the opposite side divided by the length of the adjacent side. But what happens when we come across an angle that isn't part of a right triangle? Well, that's where the concept of angles of rotation comes in. Instead of thinking about angles in terms of triangles, we can think of them as points on a circle. When we rotate a point around the center of the circle, we create an angle. By defining our trigonometric functions based on this idea of rotation, we can extend their domain beyond just acute angles. For example, let's say we want to find the sin of 150 degrees. Even though this angle isn't part of any obvious right triangle, we can still figure it out using our new understanding of angles of rotation. To do this, we would first need to convert the degree measure into radians (since that's what most calculators expect) and then use the sine function on our calculator to get the answer. But don't worry too much about those details yet - for now, just know that even seemingly impossible trig problems can often be solved with the power of angles of rotation! This measurement is often used when measuring the capacity of computer memory, hard drives, or other storage devices. he's taking not all of the walls down. Kant wrote two other books named "Critique": "Critique of the practical reason" (1788) and "Critique of the Judgement" (1790). In "Critique of the practical reason" Kant wrote about the problem of freedom and God. It was his main work of ethics. In "Critique of the Judgement" Kant wrote about beauty and teleology, or the problem if there was a purpose in general, if the world, a living creature had a reason to exist, and so on. In both books, Kant said we could not answer those problems, because they were concerned with "thing itself". In a force play, another infielder catches a ball that has touched the ground, and throws it to the first baseman. The first baseman must then touch the batter or the base with the ball before the batter can touch first base. Then the batter is out. First basemen need to have quick feet, stretch well, be quick and know how to catch wild throws. First base is one of the most important positions as a significant number of plays happen there. Optical illusions defy the old saying, "What you see is what you get." Hover over each Learning Benefit below for a detailed explanation. Fine Motor Skills What you need: - white paper - red, blue, orange, and gray construction paper - green, black, and orange tempera paints - glass of water - pencil or straw What to do: Kids love the magic of optical illusions. With your help, your child can create some simple optical illusions that will astound her friends. - Blind Spot. Explain that everyone has a place in the eye where they cannot see. On a piece of white paper, ask your child to draw an X. Approximately six inches from the X, she should draw a small circle and fill it in with her favorite color. Ask her to slowly bring the paper to her face, with the X coming toward her nose. When the circle disappears, she has found her blind spot. - Refraction. Get a pencil or a straw and a (clear) glass of water. Ask your child to examine the pencil or straw for any breaks or cracks. Then ask her to place into the glass. What does she observe? At the surface of the water, the straw or pencil should look bent or broken. (This effect is called refraction.) - After Image. Give your child two sheets of white paper and green, black, and orange tempera paints. On one sheet, ask her to use the green, black, and orange paints (in that order) to create stripes. After she has drawn several stripes, ask her to stare at her drawing for approximately 30 seconds and then quickly stare at the blank sheet of white paper. Does she now see red, white, and blue stripes? (Explain that green, black, and orange are the opposite of red, white, and blue, and that this effect is called an after image.) - Color combinations. Ask your child to cut out squares of red construction paper and glue them to the centers of blue, orange, and gray construction paper. When she looks at the red square on the blue paper, does the square seem to vibrate? Ask her to put the orange and gray sheets side by side. What does she notice about the red squares? Even though the squares were cut from the same sheet of construction paper, do they look the same now that they are against different backgrounds? - sense of sight - fine-motor skills Recommended Products for Your Child Ages 3-5 Come Out as Pansexual to Your Christian Parents ============================================== Being pansexual means that you are attracted to people regardless of their gender identity or biological sex. Coming out to your parents can be a challenging experience, especially if they have traditional religious beliefs. However, with careful planning and consideration, you can make this process smoother for both yourself and your family members. This article will provide you with a comprehensive guide on how to come out as pansexual to your Christian parents while ensuring understanding, respect, and open communication. ### Part 1: Preparation #### Step 1: Understand Your Identity Before coming out to anyone, ensure that you fully understand what being pansexual means and whether it accurately describes your feelings and attractions. It's essential to feel confident in your identity before discussing it with others. Consider researching online resources, joining support groups, talking to mental health professionals, or speaking with trusted friends who identify similarly. #### Step 2: Educate Yourself About Christianity and Homosexuality Many Christians believe homosexuality is sinful due to biblical passages such as Leviticus 18:22, Romans 1:26-27, and 1 Corinthians 6:9-10. While these verses do discuss same-gender relationships, they don't explicitly mention bisexuality, pansexuality, or other non-binary sexual orientations. Research various interpretations of these verses within different Christian denominations, including those that affirm LGBTQ+ individuals. Familiarize yourself with arguments supporting acceptance and love towards all sexual orientations based on Christian values like compassion and inclusivity (e.g., Galatians 3:28). #### Step 3: Choose the Right Time and Place Select an appropriate time and location for your conversation. Ideally, choose a quiet, private setting where you won't be interrupted or overheard by others. Make sure both you and your parents have enough time set aside to talk without feeling rushed. Avoid bringing up sensitive topics during high-stress situations, holidays, or when they may be preoccupied with other matters. #### Step 4: Plan What You Want to Say Write down your thoughts and feelings about your pansexuality. Practice explaining your orientation using clear language that emphasizes its validity and importance to you. Focus on sharing personal experiences and emotions rather than engaging in heated debates about scripture or doctrine. Anticipate potential questions or concerns your parents might raise and prepare thoughtful responses. Remember, you cannot control how they react; focus on expressing yourself honestly and calmly. #### Step 5: Seek Support From Allies [puts the cap on the marker, puts marker down reaches around for a new marker] And I think maybe I should poke my eye into my fingers. When an experiment brings two things (photons, electrons, etc.) together, they must then share a common description in quantum mechanics. When they are later separated, they keep the same quantum mechanical description or "state." In the diagram, one characteristic (e.g., "up" spin) is drawn in red, and its mate (e.g., "down" spin) is drawn in blue. The purple band means that when, e.g., two electrons are put together the pair shares both characteristics. So both electrons could show either up spin or down spin. When they are later separated, one remaining on Earth and one going to some planet of the star Alpha Centauri, they still each have both spins. In other words, each one of them can "decide" to show itself as a spin-up electron or a spin-down electron. But if later on someone measures the other one, it must "decide" to show itself as having the opposite spin. Nightspore turned his back and began to pace up and down. Adding your IHG Rewards Club card to Apple Wallet is an easy way to keep track of your rewards account and earn points when you stay at participating InterContinental Hotels Group (IHG) properties. This guide will walk you through the process of adding your IHG Rewards Club card to Apple Wallet, as well as provide some helpful tips along the way. Before we begin, make sure that: 1. You have an iPhone or iPad with iOS 8.1 or later. 2. Your device has Touch ID or Face ID capabilities. 3. The IHG Rewards Club app is installed on your device. If not, download it from the App Store. 4. You are enrolled in the IHG Rewards Club program and have your membership number handy. 5. Your IHG Reward Club account information is updated in the IHG Rewards Club mobile app. Step 1: Open the IHG Rewards Club app on your iPhone or iPad. Log into your account if prompted. This step ensures that you're accessing your correct and up-to-date IHG Rewards Club account details within the app. Having your login credentials ready streamlines the overall experience. Key tip: Make sure your device is connected to Wi-Fi or cellular data so the app can sync seamlessly with your IHG Rewards Club account. Step 2: Navigate to the Account section of the IHG Rewards Club app. Tap the menu icon (three horizontal lines) located in the upper left corner, then select 'Account.' By navigating directly to the Account tab, you avoid unnecessary searches and ensure accuracy while locating your digital IHG Rewards Club card. Step 3: Locate the Digital Membership Card area. Underneath your name and membership tier level should be a button labeled 'View My Card,' 'Digital Membership Card,' or similar wording. Tap this button. Accessing your digital membership card allows you to add it to Apple Wallet quickly and securely. Step 4: Select the option to Add to Apple Wallet. A new window may appear asking for permission to open 'Apple Wallet'. Confirm by tapping 'Open.' Integration between apps simplifies processes without compromising security. Granting permission here enables smooth communication between the IHG Rewards Club app and Apple Wallet. Step 5: Once inside Apple Wallet, review the IHG Rewards Club card details before tapping 'Add.' Verify the displayed name and membership number match those associated with your IHG Rewards Club account. Reviewing the card details guarantees precision and prevents future hassles during check-ins or point redemptions. Step 6: After successfully adding the card to Apple Wallet, locate the newly added IHG Rewards Club card via the Apple Wallet app or double-clicking the side button (or home button depending on your device model) on your iPhone to bring up wallet options. Your IHG Rewards Club card now resides safely within Apple Wallet, accessible whenever needed-no more digging around for physical cards! With these steps completed, you've successfully added your IHG Rewards Club card to Apple Wallet. Enjoy earning reward points effortlessly and managing your loyalty account efficiently. The Student will:1. identify characteristics of a tall tale 2. locate examples of exaggerations in a story 3. plan and draw a tall tale character made on paper 4. design and create paper props to accompany tall tale character 5. apply color to drawings 6. write a rough draft of an original tall tale 7. peer edit rough draft checking for inclusion of exaggerations 8. revise rough draft 9. type and print final copy of revised tall tale story then attach to the drawing 10. cut out laminated version of tall tale character with story 11. analyze and write about final version of this project 12. write how a tall tale is different from other stories Resources:1. Locate tall tale books for the classroom using the resource list of sources. 2. Show example of student tall tale character with original tall tale written on it. 3. If available, postcards with exaggerated pictures should be shown. 4. Additional activities which could be used are attached to this project. Materials:8-1/2 inch X 11 inch white computer paper Scotch tape Pencils Scissors Crayons and felt-tipped pens Sharpie pens (Black) Laminator with laminating plastic Notebook paper Dictionaries The teacher should read the class several tall tale short stories. Ask the students to point out things in the story that could not really happen. Explain that such things are called exaggerations. Discuss the characteristics of tall tales and the history of this type of story. Correlation between hard times faced by many of the pioneer settlers and some of the invented exaggerations which made humorous light of these situations could be pointed out. Weather, climates, animals, and human strength were often the topics that lent themselves to exaggeration in tall tales. Show the class a student example of the type of tall tale project that they will be making. Discuss that there are many interesting occupations or hobbies a character might have and in this tall tale their character may be something that they would not want to be in real life. The may have their character do something they may actually like to do themselves. I always have the students concentrate on making the character out of paper long before they think about writing the story. I have found that this helps them form a bond with their character. Later, they are more inspired to write the story to accompany their wonderful character. The student will:1. listen to tall tales read aloud in the classroom 2. point out examples of exaggerations found in the tall tale stories 3. brainstorm in whole class group many varied occupations 4. select an individual idea from class list 5. tape three or four sheets of white paper together to make one large sheet of paper (tape on back side only) 6. think of pose for his or her chosen character 7. think of props for chosen character 8. draw character on large sheet of paper (item 5 above) with arms up or down (teacher should point out that the body should have large area on which tall tale story can be written) 9. if character's arms are drawn sticking out or bent, attach another sheet of paper to each side of the large sheet of paper made in item 5 above 10. draw props on extra sheets of paper and tape to main figure (the props can be drawn on the same sheet as the character of there is enough room) 11. color character an props (teacher should tell students that the area on the character where story will be written must be lightly colored so story will show up) 12. using notebook paper brainstorm things his or her character might do, including exaggerations 13. lay out in numerical order the ideas he or she wants to use in the rough draft 14. plan a beginning, middle, and end of his or her tall tale 15. write a rough draft of the tall tale 16. peer edit the rough draft with at least two other students checking for the use of exaggerations, story ideas, story contents, order, spelling, capitalization, and punctuation 17. revise the rough draft after editing 18. reread draft after revision to be sure it says what was meant, and if needed, make any further changes or revisions needed for final story 19. using computer, type and print the final tall tale story for attachment to the figure of the tall tale character 20. take note of width of paper that school laminator can handle and cut off figures arms and props if page is too wide to laminate (may be taped together later) 21. give the completed character with the final typed story attached to it and any arms or props which have been cut off to fit the laminator to his or her teacher to be laminated 22. teacher will laminate character's body and any arms and/or props which were cut off to fit width of school laminator 23. teacher will return laminated items to students 24. cut out the laminated character and any separately laminated arms and/or props and tape them back together to make the whole tall tale figure 25. analyze his or her completed project by writing answers on the student evaluation sheet 26. give evaluation sheet with student part completed to teacher and teacher will complete teacher part of evaluation based on rubric 27. compare student evaluation with teacher evaluation 28. share tall tales with classmates and others What Are They? Tall tales are stories written from someone's imagination. The story can be funny or silly. They are filled with exaggerations, similes, metaphors, and lots of descriptive language. It is always told as if it were true, even though the listeners know that the story could never really happen. Some of the characters were real heroes and heroines whose stories got exaggerated a lot, others were characters such as cowboys, railroad workers, loggers, sea captains, and firefighters. Tall tales told information that really happened at the time and combined it with a wild tale full of fantasy. In general there were some bad things about tall tales. They were negative towards Native Americans, African Americans, women, and animals. The tales were about people who were trying to conquer the American Wilderness. Most of these tall tales were written back in the 1800's when the pioneers were trying to conquer the wilderness of America. It was a way for the pioneers to make the taming of America a little easier to handle. Many settlers originally came west because someone made many optimistic claims, those which told only of the good things. They were told the climate was perfect and that there was plenty of water. They were convinced that crops would spring up overnight. It was said that the soil was so fertile that even footprints would grow! In real life, living on the plains was a lot tougher that the settlers had been told. After they found out what life on the plains was really like, they had to face many hard times. It was easier to do that if a person used humor. One farmer is reputed to have said, "It could be so hot one day that the corn popped, and so cold the next day that a bucket of water would freeze so fast that the ice would still be warm." The settlers loved to exaggerate when they told tales about the huge animals, the incredible weather extremes and the monstrous fish that got away. We can thank the pioneers for tall tales. Tall tales have been associated with America's past. Tall Tales are part of our nation's oral history. We can make many connections between such tall tales and events in history. Among these are John Henry and the expansion of the railroads and Paul Bunyon and the timber industry. Tall tales were stories told and passed down from generation to generation. Tall Tale Postcards In 1902, it was popular in America to send tall tale postcards. The pictures on these cards had been changed using photo distortion, such as enlarging part of a picture without enlarging the rest of it. Some of these results were huge fruits and vegetables that were too large to fit in a wagon; giant fish that could fill a railroad flatcar; and gigantic rabbits that appeared with saddles on them. These rabbits looked in the pictures like they could be ridden as if they were horses. These cards made people laugh and were quite popular in the Great Plains states. Activities To Use With Tall TalesAfter reading several different tall tales: 1. Decide which tall tale hero or heroine you most admire; write a paper telling why. 2. Write a letter to a tall tale character telling your opinion of his or her heroic deeds. 3. Write a magazine article in which you recommend tall tales as a valuable type of literature for other students. Include personal opinions along with specific examples. 4. Using a tall tale as the basis, write a script for a puppet show. 5. Select a tall tale you have read, then write your own sequel to it. 6. Design a bulletin board which will portray a variety of tall tales. 7. Pretend that you are a friend of a tall tale hero or heroine. Tell how you handle the uniqueness of this person. 8. Write a paper telling whether it is important for children to have a hero, someone to look up to, to model themselves after. Tell whether heroes and heroines exist in our society today. 9. Prepare and give a speech about the admirable qualities of a tall tale hero or heroine. 10. Write a paper discussing current Americans who could be tall tale heroes or heroines. Be sure to specify the reasons for your selection and tell occurrences in their lives which could be humourous or exaggerated. 11. Combine two or more folk heroes or heroines into one tall tale; write a story of their adventure. Illustrate your story with something your tall tale hero or heroine might own. 12. Write a poem about your tall tale hero or heroine using the following format. Line 1 - Name the hero or heroine. Line 2 - Three present-tense verbs telling the hero's or heroine's actions. Lines 3, 4, and 5 - Three phrases telling where the hero or heroine might be found. Line 6 - A summarizing thought. Example: Paul Bunyon Chopping, clearing, hauling Over mountains Across plains Through rivers The greatest lumberjack of all. 13. Read two different versions written about the same tall tale hero or heroine. Then, write a composition telling which version you prefer and why. 14. Imagine what it would be like having this tall tale hero or heroine as your parent. Write a composition discussing the answers to the following questions. a. What do you think would be the most or least fun about having this hero or heroine as a parent? b. Do you think people would think differently of you because this hero or heroine being your parent? Would this be good or bad? c. How would your new parent affect your friendships, hobbies, or your schoolwork? d. What traits would you hope to inherit from this hero or heroine if he or she was your parent? After reading some specific stories in Cut From the Same Cloth by Robert San Souci the following activities could be done: On a U.S. map chart all the locations mentioned in all the stories used in #15-#19 then chart all of these locations mentioned in the story/ Make a map legend to identify the character. 15. For Sal Fink - Mike Fink and his wife want a husband for Sal. People used to advertise for a husband in those days. Write and advertisement pretended that it was written and illustrated by the Finks as an advertisement for their daughter. In your letter be sure to tell at least three good reasons why men would want to marry Sal. 16. For Annie Christmas - Find out what a "keelboat" is and draw a picture of one. Then write a death notice for Annie, tell the highlights of her life, and what she will be remembered for. This should be at least two paragraphs in length. List five examples of exaggeration in this story. 17. For Bess Call - Draw and color a picture of Bess based on the description of her on page 13 of the book. Then make an advertisement for a wrestling match featuring Bess's brother, Joe. Make your ad convincing by giving reasons why someone would want to attend such a match. 18. For Molly Cottontail - Molly just heard of the Woman's Rights Convention held at Seneca Falls in 1848. Pretend to be molly and write a letter to the Elizabeth Cady Stanton or Lucretia Mott and tell what she has done to prove that men and women are equal. 19. For Sweet Betsey from Pike - On a map chart the Pikes' journey on your map. Show all the places along the trail where they had trouble. Then, look on the Oregon Trail map and use it to help you plot the Trail on your map. Use Encarta to find out about the California Trail and Pilot Peak. Write a diary entry at least one page in length to tell how you feel as you travel across on this Trail. make sure you tell about the hardships along the trail and about Betsey and or Ike. Write this pretending you are one of the characters. 20. (This one does not go with the above book.) This one is to be done after you have read about a hero such as John Henry, Paul Bunyan, Mile Fink, Pecos Bill, John Chapman, or Stormalong. Draw your own picture of this character based on the description that was given in the story. Then write your own description of this character. Writing Your Own Tall Tale Think of different careers a tall tale character could have. Some of the ones you might think of include a clown, a dancer, a pirate, a king, a princess, an astronaut, a nurse and others. Examine the pictures below for ideas: You may use one other piece of computer paper to make props such as a hat or some object for your character to hold. Be sure to color your character leaving the middle of the body uncolored as the place where your story will be written. Think of a name for your tall tall character. You are now ready to write the rough draft. One way to begin is to say, you are about to hear the true story of (name of your character) or you could say (name of your character) is . . . . . You could then begin to describe your character by using some exaggerations about the size or about the special abilities of your character. Now write your rough draft. Remember, a story needs a beginning, a middle, and an ending. These tall tales should be short stories which are about one page long. Try to use details to describe things. You could use similes (as large as . . . . , as strong as . . . . ,) and you could compare it to some real exaggerated topic. Read your story to a friend. Sometimes when you hear your own story, you may realize that you left something out or that you need to add something to your story. Ask your friend if there is anything you could do to make your story better. Check your rough drafts to see if you described you character and told at least two or three exaggerations either about your character's appearance or about something your character can do. When your rough draft has been checked by a friend or parent, ask your teacher to check it. After your teacher has checked your rough draft, you are ready to type the final version and attach it to your character. After you have typed your whole tall tale, write Written by: and your name on the bottom left of the figure. Your tall tale is now finished and ready to be displayed and enjoyed by everyone. You may even want to write other tall tales. These are fun to make! Tall Tale Resource List! Cut From The Same Cloth: American Women of Myth, Legend, and Tall Tale by Robert San Souci ! The Elephant's Bathtub: Wonder Tales From The Far East by Frances Carpenter ! Old Stormalong, John Henry, or Paul Bunyan by deLeeuw The Statue of Liberty My name is the Statue of Liberty. I'm so tall that I can see half around the world. I'm so tall, I can even see the ocean. So, here's my story . It was late one night, nobody was around but me, so I decided to hop off the pedestal and go into the ocean. I picked up my long dress and step by step went into the ocean. It was cold, but I went on. I saw lots of fish, seaweed, seashells, and rocks. It was so colorful down there with all those fish. I wouldn't have been able to see without my torch. One of the fish, an Angel fish, came to me and said, "What on earth are you?" "Why, I'm the Statue of Liberty!" "Oh sure, whatever, well I'm Bubbles. How did you get down here?" she asked. "Oh," said Bubbles. "Have you ever been in the ocean before?" "No, but I look at it every day," I said. "Well, it was nice down here while it lasted," I said. "Bye." "Bye," said Bubbles. After Bubbles swam away I walked out of the ocean and got back on the pedestal the place where I was before. Written by: Hillary The Soccer Player I was getting ready for a soccer game. I started stretching. My legs stretched so far they got stuck on a branch in a tree. I ended upside down with my arms folded. By the time I got down, it was time to start the game. The kids in the other team looked pretty scary. I got to kick the ball first. I kicked so hard, it ended up at Furman University! The kids there started playing with our ball too. Except, they were playing baseball. The batter hit the soccer ball and got a homerun! Then, we got the ball back. When we got back to the game, I kicked the ball into the goal! It bounced back and I kicked it back in. This kept going on until the end of the game. Everyone cheered for me. I began to stretch my arms. Suddenly I was stuck on the top of a tree. Explain why you think your project is or is not successful. 7. Explain what makes a tall tale different from other stories.
