[puts the yellow ball on the base] Title: How to Delete Your Ebates Account Ebates is a popular cashback shopping website that offers rewards for online purchases made through their links. However, if you've decided that you no longer want to use Ebates or need to close your account for any reason, this comprehensive guide will walk you through the process of deleting your Ebates account. ### Part 1: Preparation Before starting the deletion process, there are some essential steps to take care of first: #### Step 1: Redeem Any Cash Back Earnings Key Tip: Make sure you have redeemed all available cash back earnings before closing your account. Once deleted, you cannot recover these funds. Here's how to redeem your earnings: 1. Log into your Ebates account. 2. Click on your account name at the top right corner of the page. 3. Select "My Account" from the dropdown menu. 4. Go to the "Payments & Rewards" tab. 5. Choose between receiving payment via check or PayPal. If opting for PayPal, ensure your linked email address matches your PayPal account. 6. Confirm your preferred payout method by clicking "Save Changes." The minimum threshold for redemption is $5.01. 7. Wait for the next scheduled payout date (usually every three months). #### Step 2: Unsubscribe From Marketing Emails Guideline: To avoid future promotional emails after deleting your Ebates account, unsubscribe from marketing communications now. This ensures that once the account is closed, you won't receive unwanted emails. 1. Check your inbox for recent Ebates emails. 2. Scroll down to locate the "Unsubscribe" link, typically found near the bottom of the message. 3. Click the link and confirm your decision to stop receiving promotional emails. ### Part 2: Deleting Your Ebates Account Now that you've prepared for the deletion process, follow these steps to delete your Ebates account permanently: #### Step 1: Access My Account Page 1. Visit the Ebates homepage (www.ebates.com) and click "Sign In" at the upper-right corner. 2. Enter your login credentials and click "Secure Sign In." #### Step 2: Navigate to Settings 1. After signing in, hover over your account name at the top-right corner again but do not click yet. 2. A new dropdown list should appear; select "Settings" from the options provided. #### Step 3: Close Your Account Permanently 1. On the left sidebar, find and click "Close my account." 2. You will be redirected to another page with important information regarding the consequences of account closure. Take time to read through them carefully. 3. At the bottom of the page, tick both boxes beside the statements: "I understand I am giving up my membership status, including all earned cash back," and "I acknowledge that I can never get my account back once it has been closed." 4. Finally, press the big red button labeled "Continue to deactivate my account". Congratulations! You have successfully deleted your Ebates account following our extensive guide. Remember always to consider alternative ways of earning cash back or using similar services responsibly while protecting your privacy and personal data. huh, we have to put it in here you want fishes? [a barn full of plastic farm animals] Putting on a Play 4th Grade Oral Language Resources Students will: Learn about putting on play. Access prior knowledge and build background about the significance of putting on a play. Explore and apply the concept of putting on a play. Students will: Demonstrate an understanding of putting on a play. Orally use words that describe the people in a play. Extend oral vocabulary by speaking about how to put on a play. Use key concept words [play, actors, actresses, narrator, director, role, audition, cast; rehearse, nervousness, playwright]. Explain Use the slideshow to review the key concept words. Explain that students are going to learn about putting on a play: What is a play. Who is involved in a play. How does a play work. The purpose of a play. Model After the host introduces the slideshow, point to the photo on screen. Ask students: What do you see in this photo? (billboards, lights, ads, etc.) What are the ads about? (plays). Ask students: What play do you want to see? (answers will vary). Say: A play is a story written to be acted out on stage. There are many people who are involved in a play. Many people go to see plays because of the people in them or because of the story. Have you ever seen or been in a school play? (answers will vary). Guided Practice Guide students through the next three slides, showing them the different people involved in a play. Always have the students discuss what the people are doing. Apply Play the games that follow. Have them discuss with their partner the different topics that appear during the Talk About It feature. After the first game, ask students to talk about their favorite actors and actresses. After the second game, have them discuss the hardest and easiest emotion to show. Close Ask students: How are plays different from movies? Explain. Summarize for students that plays are great a form of entertainment, just like movies. Both plays and movies involve lots of people, props, and stage settings. However, plays are different from movies since people are acting live and sometimes there is only one set for all the scenes, whereas in the movies, scenes happen in many different places. Encourage them to think about whether they like plays or movies. [music starts in the background I think from the TV] [about the shapes NEA has in his hands] Or do you want to draw another pumpkin? [2.2 sec, rustles, S murmurs too softly to understand] Probably the most widely accepted theory explaining the evolution of larval stages is the need for "dispersal". Sessile organisms such as barnacles and tunicates, and sea-floor groups like mussels and crabs, need some way to move their young into new territory, since they cannot move long distances as adults. Many species have relatively long pelagic larval stages (how long a larva is in the water column). During this time, larvae feed and grow, and many species move through several stages of development. For example, most barnacles moult through six "nauplius" larva stages before moulting to a "cipris", when they look to settle. The larvae eat different food from the adults, and disperse. Title: Find and Replace on Microsoft Excel (PC and Mac) Introduction: This tutorial will guide you through the process of using the "Find and Replace" function in Microsoft Excel for both PC and Mac users. This feature is useful when you want to locate specific data within your workbook or replace certain text with new information. It's an efficient way to make global changes across multiple sheets and cells without having to manually search and edit them individually. We will break down this task into several manageable steps, providing clear instructions along with helpful tips and guidelines. Step 1: Open Your Excel Workbook On either your PC or Mac, launch Microsoft Excel and open the workbook containing the data that requires find and replace operations. Make sure you have enabled editing by clicking 'Enable Editing' if prompted at the top bar. Step 2: Accessing the Find and Replace Dialog Box To initiate the Find and Replace operation, click on the 'Home' tab located at the top left corner of the ribbon interface. Within the 'Editing' group, select 'Find & Select'. A drop-down menu appears; from there, choose 'Replace...' which opens up the Find and Replace dialog box. Alternatively, press Ctrl + H (Command + Shift + H on Mac) as a shortcut. Step 3: Understanding the Interface The Find what field allows you to input the text or value you wish to locate. The Replace with field lets you specify what you would like to insert instead. Preview areas below these fields display found matches and allow testing potential replacements before committing to changes. Key Tips: * Case sensitivity can be toggled using the 'Match case' checkbox. When checked, only identical cases will be detected. * To look for whole cell values rather than partial content, tick the 'Match entire cell contents' option. * Use the 'Look in' dropdown to determine whether Excel should scan formulas, comments, or constants. Step 4: Locating Data Enter the text or value you are looking for in the 'Find what' field. Then hit 'Find Next'. If a match is discovered, its location will be highlighted within your spreadsheet. Continue pressing 'Find Next' until all instances have been reviewed. Step 5: Performing Replacements After ensuring you're comfortable with locating individual occurrences, proceed to entering your replacement text in the 'Replace with' field. Click on 'Replace All' if you want to modify every instance simultaneously. Otherwise, navigate between results using 'Find Next', replacing selected entries one-by-one via 'Replace'. Remember to review each change carefully to avoid unintended alterations. Step 6: Confirmation Message and Undo Option Once completed, a confirmation message shows the number of replacements made. You also receive an alert if no matching items were found. Should any errors arise during the procedure, remember that you can undo everything by pressing Ctrl+Z (Command+Z on Mac). Conclusion: Congratulations! By following this comprehensive tutorial, you now possess the knowledge required to effectively utilize Excel's powerful Find and Replace functionality. With practice, executing such tasks becomes second nature, saving time and reducing manual labor significantly. Happy analyzing! The emperor would notice an outstanding young man from one of the best families. He would adopt him as his son. Title: How to Crochet a Wide Flat Tape Edging Introduction: This crocheted tape edging is both beautiful and functional, making it perfect for adding a decorative touch to various items such as tablecloths, napkins, or even clothing. This wide flat tape edging uses basic stitches, so if you're new to crochet, this project will help build your skills while creating something lovely. By following these clear steps with helpful tips along the way, you can create professional-looking results at home! Materials needed: * A crochet hook (size F-5 or G-6) * Yarn in any color(s) of your choice; lace weight yarn recommended for delicate appearance * Scissors * Yarn needle 1. Chain Stitch Setup: Begin by chaining an odd number of chains using your chosen yarn and crochet hook. The length of the chain determines the width of the finished band; aim for 20-30 chains for a good starting point. Make sure all loops are uniformly sized and snug but not too tight. Key tip: An odd number of chains allows for seamless joining when working back along the row. 2. Single Crochet Foundation Row: Turn work over and single crochet (sc) into second chain from the hook, then continue sc across all remaining chains. When complete, chain one (ch1), which acts as a turning chain, ready for the next row. Remember that each single crochet counts as one loop plus one chain space. Guideline: Keep tension consistent throughout foundation row to ensure straight edges later on. 3. Double Crochet First Edge Row: Now we start building height with double crochet (dc). Ch1, skip first stitch (the initial sc), then dc into next stitch. Continue down the entire row until only one stitch remains before end of row. Dc into final stitch, ch1, turn work over. Note: Skipping the first stitch creates a neater edge finish. 4. Half Double Crochet Body Rows: For the main body of the tape, perform half double crochet (hdc) rows. Hdc in every stitch across, including the top of the beginning and ending chains. At the end of each row, hdc twice into last stitch, chain one, then turn work over. Repeat this process until desired length reached. Tip: Counting rows regularly ensures accurate measurement and prevents mistakes during assembly. 5. Second Edge Row - Return Pass: After completing the required number of body rows, begin returning along original foundation row. Perform a series of slip stitches (sl st) through both loops of previous rows' corresponding stitches. Start just after the turning chain of the foundation row, moving left to right. Slip stitch continuously without chaining between each stitch. Once completed, fasten off yarn securely. Important note: Ensuring equal spacing between sl st ensures symmetry and neatness. 6. Weaving Ends & Finalizing Edges: Using a yarn needle, carefully thread leftover yarn tails through adjacent stitches on either side of where they were cut. Pull gently to close gaps, trim excess threads. Press lightly under a warm iron (with cloth protector) to set stitches and enhance smoothness. Congratulations! You have successfully created a stunning crocheted wide flat tape edging! Remember practice makes perfect, especially when learning new techniques like those presented herein. Happy crafting! Come Back from Summer Vacation: A Comprehensive Guide ===================================================== Introduction ------------ Summer vacation is often filled with relaxation, adventure, and excitement. However, coming back from summer break can be challenging as you transition into a new routine, especially if you've been away for an extended period. This comprehensive guide will help you come back from your summer vacation feeling refreshed, organized, and prepared for the upcoming season. By following these steps, you'll ensure a smooth reentry into your daily life while maintaining the positive energy gained during your time off. ### Step 1: Plan Your Return #### Explanation Before physically returning home or to work, take some time to plan your arrival. This includes scheduling any necessary appointments (e.g., doctor, dentist), arranging transportation, and setting up accommodations if needed. Planning ahead allows you to ease into your regular schedule without experiencing undue stress upon arrival. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Set aside ample time between travel dates and important obligations to avoid rushing. * Make a list of tasks that need immediate attention after your return. * Consider using a digital calendar or planner to organize your commitments. ### Step 2: Prepare Your Living Space #### Explanation A clean and organized living space promotes productivity and reduces anxiety. Before leaving for vacation, tidy up your home by decluttering surfaces, washing dishes, doing laundry, and taking out trash. Additionally, make sure essential appliances like your refrigerator are cleaned thoroughly to prevent spoiled food or unpleasant odors. Upon your return, spend some time organizing personal items, putting away luggage, and restocking supplies. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Create designated spaces for belongings to reduce clutter. * Establish a regular cleaning routine to maintain order. * Dispose of expired foods and refresh perishable items. ### Step 3: Reconnect With Loved Ones #### Explanation During summer vacation, it's common to become disconnected from friends and family due to travel schedules or distance. As soon as possible, reach out to loved ones and share stories about your experiences. Schedule get-togethers or phone calls to strengthen relationships and catch up on missed events. #### Key Tips & Guidelines * Be intentional when reaching out to others; prioritize those who matter most. * Share photos and memories from your trip to enhance conversations. * Organize group activities to maximize connection opportunities. ### Step 4: Review Work Commitments #### Explanation [as MOT begins to move the box of paper] He can think of very sneaky ideas. The most important is the redshift of very far away galaxies. Redshift is the Doppler effect occurring in light. When an object moves away from Earth, its color rays look more similar to the color red than they actually are, because the movement stretches the wavelength of light given off by the object. Scientists use the word "red hot" to describe this stretched light wave because red is the longest wavelength on the visible spectrum. The more redshift there is, the faster the object is moving away. By measuring the redshift, scientists proved that the universe is expanding, and they can work out how fast the object is moving away from the Earth. With very exact observation and measurements, scientists believe that the universe was a singularity approximately 13.8 billion years ago. Because most things become colder as they expand, scientists assume that the universe was very small and very hot when it started. Heart is a rock band which started in Seattle in the 1960s. Sometimes, if the temperature is low enough it can go through deposition, where it changes straight to a solid. Normally a gas must first condense to a liquid, and then freeze to become a solid, but if the temperature is very low it can skip the liquid stage and instantly become solid. Frost on the ground in winter is caused by this. Water vapour (a gas) goes into the air which is very cold, and instantly becomes ice due to deposition. Buzz [child laughs] whoops [! Burning of wood is a chemical change as new substances which cannot be changed back (e.g. carbon dioxide) are formed. For example, if wood is burned in a fireplace, there is not wood anymore but ash. Other examples include burning of a candle, rusting of iron, baking a cake, etc. Special details that describe how a chemical change takes place are called chemical properties. After the electrical impulse goes through the AV Node, the electrical impulse will go through the "conduction system" of the ventricle. Conduction means heat or electricity traveling through something. This brings the electrical impulse to the ventricles. The first part of the conduction system is the "bundle of His". "His" is named for the doctor (Wilhelm His, Jr) who discovered it. Bundle means strings or wires grouped together in parallel. Once the bundle ("meaning a group of strings or wires going in parallel directions") goes through the ventricle muscle, it divides into two "bundle branches", the left bundle branch and the right bundle branch. The left bundle branch travels to the left ventricle and the right bundle branch travels to the right ventricle. At the end of the bundle branches, the electrical impulse goes into the ventricular muscle through the Purkinje Fibers. This is what makes ventricle contraction take place and makes ventricular systole. Then there was a mom of the Martian family a dad of the Martian family a little girl and a dog. Title: Be a Gracious Guest When You're Vegetarian and the Host Isn't Introduction: As a vegetarian, attending social gatherings or dining at someone's house can sometimes be challenging, especially if your host isn't familiar with vegetarian diets. However, being a gracious guest involves respecting your host's efforts while ensuring your dietary needs are met. This tutorial will provide you with steps to navigate such situations gracefully. Step 1: Inform Your Host About Your Dietary Preferences Ahead of Time Key Tips: * Notify them as soon as possible, ideally when you RSVP. * Be polite and appreciative; remember they're going out of their way to accommodate you. * Offer suggestions if needed but don't insist. Desired Outcome: Giving your host ample time to prepare allows them to create or buy food that suits your diet without feeling overwhelmed. It also shows consideration for their time and effort. Step 2: Show Appreciation for Their Hospitality Key Tips: * Compliment the meal even if it doesn't cater specifically to your preferences. * Engage in conversations unrelated to food. Desired Outcome: Expressing gratitude makes your host feel valued and respected. It keeps the focus on enjoying each other's company rather than centering on what you can't eat. Step 3: Bring a Dish to Share Key Tips: * Choose something delicious yet simple. * Make sure it complements the existing menu. * Check for common allergens in advance. Desired Outcome: Sharing a dish ensures you have something satisfying to eat and demonstrates initiative. Moreover, it could introduce others to new flavors and expand their culinary horizons. Step 4: Eat What You Can Key Tips: * Identify side dishes or ingredients that align with your diet. * Avoid making negative comments about the food. * If unsure, ask your host about specific items. Desired Outcome: By eating a variety of available foods, you avoid drawing unnecessary attention to your dietary restrictions. Plus, asking questions opens up opportunities for education and understanding between you and your host. Step 5: Respect Cultural Norms Surrounding Food Key Tips: * Understand that some cultures view hospitality through offering food. * Refrain from refusing food unless there is a health-related reason. * Explain gently why certain foods aren't suitable instead of simply declining. Desired Outcome: Being mindful of cultural nuances prevents misunderstanding and fosters mutual respect among guests and hosts. Step 6: Don't Preach About Veganism/Vegetarianism Key Tips: * Remember that everyone has different beliefs and lifestyles. * Focus on enjoying the event rather than converting people to your lifestyle choice. * Answer questions honestly if asked directly. Desired Outcome: Maintaining an open-minded attitude creates a positive atmosphere where everyone feels comfortable expressing themselves. Conclusion: Following these steps enables you to enjoy social events as a gracious vegetarian guest. Always keep in mind that communication, appreciation, flexibility, and respect go a long way towards creating harmonious interactions around shared meals. You don't put nails in like that. Once upon a time, there were two boys who were very brave. They often went on adventures together. One day, the boys dared each other to go into a dark cave. They went together, but they were scared. The boys made it to the cave's entrance and paused. They looked at each other and the taller boy said, "We have to go in there? That is a difficult fight!" The smaller boy nodded, but neither of them wanted to take a step forward. They both knew something was waiting on the other side of the cave. Suddenly, the smaller boy stumbled and fell into the cave. He screamed and the taller boy had to help him. Then they knew they had to go forward. The smaller boy was afraid and begged not to go, but the taller boy had no choice. He had to help his friend. So, the boys continued further into the dark cave, looking for the scary thing that was waiting for them. When they made it to the other side, they were faced with two large dragons. The boys tried to fight their way out, but it was too difficult. The dragons defeated them and the boys knew they had lost the fight. The boys never returned from the dark cave again. (Optionally, once students are comfortable with irregular words, you can include some regular words in the pack too.) - Now let's play a game. We're going to try to go through this stack of cards as fast as we can saying the word on each card. Let's see how fast we can go. My turn first. - Next, model taking the top card of the stack, showing it to the students, and saying the word after a pause. Continue through the stack. - Do you think you can go faster than I did? Call on a single student in the group, starting with a student you think may be slower. Show the first card: What's the word? If the student is incorrect, correct him, have him repeat your answer, and move to the next card. Praise correct answers. - Select the next quicker student and repeat until all students in the group have worked through the stack. - Okay, now let's go faster. Shuffle the stack of cards and repeat with students in the same order, but encouraging them to go faster. - If time and focus allow, shuffle and repeat at an even faster pace. - For students who struggle, give them help and make a note in an Activity Log. The method compares words in a text to a list of words which are familiar in everyday writing. The words that are not on the list are called "unfamiliar". The number of words per sentence are counted. This number and the percentage of unfamiliar words is put into a formula. The result is a reading age. Someone of this age should be able to read the text. Click on the image to enlarge it. Click again to close. Download PDF (197 KB) These are level 4 and 5 number problems from the Figure It Out series. use ratios to solve problems (Problem 1) use addition facts and logic to solve puzzles (Problem 2 and 4) In the gear train shown, cog B acts only as a transmitter. As cog A turns one turn anticlockwise, cog B will make two turns clockwise. As cog B makes two turns clockwise, cog C will be driven to turn 11/2 turns anticlockwise. This happens because cog B has three-quarters the number of sprockets as cog C, so two turns of cog B make 1 1/2 turns of cog C. Therefore, in four turns of cog A, cog C will turn 1 1/2 as many turns. 4 x 1 1/2 = 6 turns Gear train relationships are easily observed in construction toys. They occur in many everyday situations, particularly in cars, bicycles, and motor-driven machinery. Many students will find making a paper copy of the dominoes useful so that they can move them around to find a solution. Students who know the way dominoes are numbered should be able to use this logically to find a solution. If the four sides each have 12 dots, this gives a sum of 4 x 12 = 48 dots. The dots in each corner square are counted twice to reach this 48-dot sum. In total, there are 28 dots on the four dominoes. The corner squares must account for the difference of 20 dots (48 - 28). Working on this premise, the only numbers that can go in the corner square are 5, 4, 5, and 6. Students can place the corner dots and then work out their solution knowing that the side total is 12 dots. This traditional problem provides the ideal opportunity to use an act-it-out strategy. Students can assume the role of the characters and try out possible solutions. Alternatively, students could use counters for the characters or use some form of recording strategy. For example: The only possible first move is to take the chicken across because all other trips leave a disastrous couple behind. Two possible moves can occur in this next move: either the farmer returns to take the fox across or he returns to take the bag of corn across. The next move is to bring the chicken back as it cannot be left with either the fox or the bag of corn. In either case, the chicken is brought back, the fox or the corn is taken across, and the farmer returns to pick up the chicken. The problem can be acted out with cut-down plastic drink bottles used as the buckets. A systematic recording strategy is needed to keep track of the quantities. An important piece of reasoning is that the only way that 5 litres can be measured is if 2 litres are already in the 3 litre bucket or there is a way of pouring exactly 2 litres from a full 7 litre bucket. Encourage students to use a structured recording system such as the one shown below: Below are two possible solution strategies that use this recording system. Answers to Problems 1. a. 6 full turns 2. One solution: 3. One solution: The farmer takes the chicken to the other side of the river and leaves it there. He goes back and gets the fox. He leaves the fox on the other side of the river and takes the chicken back. He leaves the chicken on the bank, takes the corn over to the other side, and leaves it with the fox. He then goes back and fetches the chicken. Another solution follows the above for moving the chicken, but the trips for the corn and the fox are reversed. 4. One solution is to follow these steps: Fill the 7 L container. From the 7 L container, fill the 3 L container and then empty the 3 L container. Do this twice. There is now 1 L remaining in the 7 L container. Pour the remaining litre into the 3 L container. Refill the 7 L container. From the 7 L container, top up the 3 L container until it is full. There are now exactly 5 L of water in the 7 L container. Fill the 3 L container. Pour this 3 L into the 7 L container. Do this twice. (There are now 6 L in the 7 L container.) Fill the 3 L container. Top up the 7 L container. This leaves 2 L in the 3 L Empty the 7 L container. Pour the remaining 2 L from the 3 L container into the 7 L container. Fill up the 3 L container again. Add the 3 L from the 3 L container to the 2 L in the 7 L container. There are now exactly 5 L of water in the 7 L container. You may find other solutions. For example, let's say a mother only has genes for brown hair and a father only has genes for red hair. The child will inherit - receive - genes for red hair (from her father) and brown hair (from her mother). The brown hair gene is 'dominant' to the red hair gene. This means the child will have brown hair even though she has genes for both red and brown hair. This means only one dominant gene is needed for the child to receive that particular trait, while two recessive genes are needed for one. Bodies that are able to attract or repel things in this way are said to be 'electrified', or to be 'charged with electricity'. When two different substances are rubbed together, an electrical charge is produced because one of them will give electrons to the other. The reason is that the atoms in the two substances have unequal power to attract electrons. So the one that is more able to attract electrons will take electrons from the one that has a lower attractive force. If glass is rubbed against something else, it may either give or take electrons. What happens depends on what the other thing is. The goat climbed up and picked the bananas. He gave the ripe ones to the tiger and the tiger had a good meal. The goat went hungry. So XXX like the baby and the baby bottles yeah. At high pressures, oxygen gas (O) can be dangerous to animals, including humans. It can cause convulsions and other health problems. Oxygen toxicity usually begins to occur at pressures more than 50 kilopascals (kPa), equal to about 50% oxygen in the air at standard pressure (air on Earth has around 20% oxygen). The following is a list of various activites the teacher can choose from when teaching a unit on firefighters. 1. The teacher will read the book "The Fire Station Book" by Nancy Bundt. 2. The teacher and the student will discuss ways the firefighter helps the community. 3. Have the children discuss what they would do in an emergency fire situation. Discuss ways to prevent fires and what to do in an actual emergency. Discuss using 911. 4. Have a fire drill. Once outside, find a grassy area and talk with the children about how they should "Stop, Drop, and Roll". Have the children stop what they are doing, drop to the ground, and roll over and over until the pretend flames are out. 1. Firefighter Hat - Supply each student with a clean, one-gallon bleach bottle - pre-cut to fit their head. Allow the student to use red and black tempera paint to decorate their own personalized firefighter helmet. When dry, encourage the children to wear their helmets and role play being firefighters. 2. Construction Paper Fire Truck - Hand out precut rectangular and circle shapes. Let the children glue the shapes on to constrction paper to design their own fire trucks. Use markers and/or crayons to add details. 1. Bucket Brigade (from Terrific Topics:Careers) Divide the class into two teams and have the students in each team form a line. For each team, set one large bucket filled with water at one end of the line, and an empty bucket at the other end of the line. Give each team a large cup. Explain that, when playing the game, the children cannot move from their positions. Members from each team have to work together and use the cup to pass water from the full bucket to the empty one. The first team to move all of its water to the empty bucket wins! 2. Fire Station Play Center Set up an area of the room to represent a fire station. Supply it with items such as firefighters' hats, boots, jackets, gloves, flashlights, a hose, toy fire trucks, a bell, telephones, and pails. Let the children pretend to be firefighters. 1. I'm a Firefighter (by Judy Hall) Sung to: "I'm a Little Teapot" I'm a firefighter Dressed in red, With my fire hat On my head. I can drive the fire truck, Fight fires, too, And help to make things Safe for you. 2. Down at the Firehouse (by Jean Warren) Sung to: "Down by the Station" Down at the firehouse Early in the morning, You can see our clothes Hanging in a row. When there is a fire, We can dress real fast. Boots, jackets, hats, gloves, Off we go! FIELD TRIPS/RESOURCE VISITORS 1. Arrange a tour of a fire station. Point out the different equipment the firefighters use. 2. Invite a firefighter from a local station to come talk with the students. (Hopefully the firefighter can come on a fire truck) Ask the firefighter to bring his/her special clothing and gear. So where do you think he went to look for a rocket? Some very beautiful music was written for use in church services (sacred music) by the Italian composer Giovanni da Palestrina (1525-1594). In Palestrina's music, many singers sing together (this is called a choir). There was also plenty of music not written for the church, such as happy dance music and romantic love songs. Popular instruments during the Renaissance included the viols (a string instrument played with a bow), lutes (a plucked stringed instrument that is a little like a guitar), and the virginal, a small, quiet keyboard instrument. Title: How to Be a Successful Party Planner ========================================== Becoming a party planner requires strong organizational skills, creativity, attention to detail, and excellent communication abilities. This comprehensive guide will walk you through all aspects of planning successful parties, from setting your goals and creating a budget to executing every element flawlessly. By following these steps, you'll be well-equipped to plan any type of celebration that meets-and exceeds-your clients' expectations. ### Part 1: Setting Your Goals and Creating a Plan #### Step 1: Define Your Niche *Explanation*: Identifying your target market allows you to focus your efforts and resources effectively, ensuring success as a party planner. *Key Tips*: Consider specializing in specific types of events such as corporate gatherings, children's birthday celebrations, or milestone occasions like weddings and anniversaries. Research local competition and assess where there is room for growth. #### Step 2: Develop a Business Plan *Explanation*: A solid business plan provides structure and direction when starting your new venture. It outlines important details including mission statement, services offered, marketing strategies, pricing structures, and financial projections. *Key Guidelines*: Clearly define your objectives, identify potential challenges, and establish methods for measuring progress. Regularly review and update this document as needed. #### Step 3: Establish Legal Entities and Register Your Business *Explanation*: Protect yourself by registering your business with appropriate government agencies and obtaining necessary licenses and permits. Consult legal professionals if unsure about requirements. *Key Tips*: Choose an available name that reflects your brand identity and consider forming a limited liability company (LLC) to safeguard personal assets. #### Step 4: Create a Budget *Explanation*: Understanding projected income and expenses ensures responsible management of finances during the early stages of building your client base. *Key Guidelines*: Factor in fixed costs like office space, insurance, and software subscriptions; variable expenses related to event production; and marketing investments. Set aside funds for emergencies and ongoing professional development. ### Part 2: Building Client Relationships #### Step 5: Network Within Your Community *Explanation*: Connecting with other industry professionals expands your network and generates referrals, leading to increased opportunities for collaboration and growth. *Key Tips*: Attend conferences, workshops, and networking events; join online communities and social media groups; volunteer at charity functions. Cultivate genuine relationships based on trust and mutual respect.
